const response = await docClient.send(command); return { statusCode: 200, headers, body: JSON.stringify(response.Items || []) }; } if (pathParameters.id && path === `/quiz/${pathParameters.id}/attempts` && method === "POST") { const body = JSON.parse(event.body || "{}"); const quizId = pathParameters.id; const attemptId = `att_${Date.now()}`; const quizRes = await docClient.send(new GetCommand({ TableName: QUIZ_TABLE, Key: { userId, quizId } })); if (!quizRes.Item) return { statusCode: 404, headers, body: JSON.stringify({ message: "Không tìm thấy bài quiz" }) }; const questions = quizRes.Item.questions || []; const userAnswers = body.answers || []; let score = 0; const gradedQuestions = questions.map((q, idx) => { // Lấy linh hoạt cả 2 kiểu đặt tên biến const correctIdx = q.answer_index ?? q.answerIndex; const isCorrect = userAnswers[idx] === correctIdx; if (isCorrect) score += 1; return { ...q, user_answer: userAnswers[idx], is_correct: isCorrect }; }); const attemptItem = { userId, attemptId, quizId, score, totalQuestions: questions.length, answers: gradedQuestions, submittedAt: new Date().toISOString(), }; await docClient.send(new PutCommand({ TableName: QUIZ_ATTEMPT_TABLE, Item: attemptItem })); return { statusCode: 200, headers, body: JSON.stringify(attemptItem) }; } return { statusCode: 404, headers, body: JSON.stringify({ message: "Route không tồn tại" }), }; } catch (error) { console.error("Lỗi Lambda:", error); return { statusCode: 500, headers, body: JSON.stringify({ message: "Lỗi xử lý hệ thống", error: error.message }), }; } };

title: Introduction
content:
Architecture flow This workshop builds an AI-powered learning journal application on AWS. The user accesses the frontend through CloudFront, AWS WAF protects the public edge, Amplify hosts the web app, and Cognito authenticates the user before the frontend calls protected API Gateway routes.
Request path The user opens the frontend application. AWS WAF filters unwanted traffic before it reaches CloudFront. CloudFront serves the SPA assets from Amplify. Cognito authenticates the user and returns JWT tokens. The SPA calls API Gateway with the access token. API Gateway forwards requests to the main Lambda API. Lambda stores or retrieves data from DynamoDB. Lambda creates presigned S3 URLs for image upload and download. Long-running AI jobs are sent to SQS for asynchronous processing. Failed jobs are routed to a DLQ for troubleshooting. SQS triggers the Lambda AI Worker. The worker calls Groq for AI completion. The worker saves reports, quizzes, and summaries in DynamoDB. CloudWatch, SNS, and AWS Budgets provide logs, alerts, and cost guardrails. Main components Amazon Cognito handles sign-up, sign-in, Google federation, and JWT-based authorization. API Gateway + Lambda API expose routes for health checks, profile info, journal CRUD, image upload, AI reports, quizzes, search, and analytics. DynamoDB stores journal logs, AI reports, quizzes, quiz attempts, and analytics-friendly data. S3 stores uploaded learning images while access is controlled through presigned URLs. SQS + Lambda Worker decouple AI generation from user-facing API latency. Groq API provides fast Llama model completion for reports and quizzes.

title: Prepare the environment
content:
Prepare AI data tables and queues AI DynamoDB tables If you did not create these tables in the previous section, create them now:
AiReports Partition key: userId Sort key: reportId Quizzes Partition key: userId Sort key: quizId QuizAttempts Partition key: userId Sort key: attemptId Recommended status values:
pending processing completed failed SQS queues Open Amazon SQS and create:
ai-report-jobs for report generation jobs. quiz-jobs for quiz generation jobs. ai-jobs-dlq or separate DLQs for failed messages. For each main queue:
Use Standard queue. Configure a visibility timeout longer than the expected worker processing time. Attach the DLQ with a maximum receive count such as 3. IAM permissions The main Lambda API needs permission to send messages to the two main queues. The worker needs permission to:
Receive and delete messages from SQS. Read journal data from DynamoDB. Write AI results to AiReports, Quizzes, and QuizAttempts. Write logs to CloudWatch. Read the Groq key from environment variables or Secrets Manager.

title: Worklog
Event 2
content:
Summary Report: “AWS FIRST CLOUD AI JOURNEY COMMUNITY DAY 23/05/2026” 1. Context Is Everything" - Making AI Actually Work for You This talk is part of a series of technology-sharing events organized by Amazon Web Services (AWS), focusing on the topic of Context Engineering — the art of providing the right context to maximize the power of AI models.
Speakers Tinh Truong – Platform Engineer tại GoTymeX. Summary of content and main activities The talk revolved around the core message: “Context Is Everything” — AI models are already powerful, but the input (context) is the bottleneck that determines the quality of the output. The speaker presented four main ideas to help students use AI more effectively:
Activity 1 — Why AI fails when it lacks context
Learn about common mistakes when asking questions to AI: answers that are too general, misdirected, overly verbose, and ignore constraints. The lesson: AI cannot read the mind of users — AI only uses exactly what you provide. Activity 2 — Defining “Context” in AI
Analyze the four components of a quality context: (1) Your goal, (2) Context (student, beginner, group project, deadline), (3) Constraints (tech stack, style, format), (4) Relevant evidence (code, docs, examples). Activity 3 — Three common mistakes when using AI
Mistake #1: “Internet Puller” — dumping entire articles, PDFs, and screenshots into a single chat. Mistake #2: Telling AI things it already knows. Mistake #3: Lack of goals and constraints — resulting in vague prompts and ambiguous responses. Activity 4 — From Prompt → Context → Memory
Learn how AI usage is evolving: from a single question → grounded with documentation → personalized over time. Explore the concept of a “Second AI Brain” and the Store → Retrieve → Generate → Learn process. Activity 5 — Suggesting student-friendly AI projects
The speaker suggested four projects for students to start: AI Study Assistant (summarizing notes, creating quizzes), PDF Chat App (Q&A based on study materials), AI Code Reviewer (explaining bugs, suggesting improvements), and Personal Second Brain (storing and retrieving notes). Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Understand that having “many contexts” isn’t necessarily better — what matters is a quality context that’s focused and relevant to the specific goal.
A good prompt must have four elements: Goal, Audience, Constraints, and Success criteria — this is the skill of “communicating” with AI just like communicating with humans.
Context engineering will be a core skill in the future, not just prompt engineering.
AI is a tool for scaling capabilities — students should start building their own products today instead of just reading theory.
Newly Acquired Skills
Technical Skills: Knowledge of designing structured prompts with a Goal — Context — Constraints — Output format. Familiarity with RAG concepts, vector databases, Bedrock, and the Store → Retrieve → Generate → Learn process.
Critical Thinking: Ability to distinguish between generic, grounded, and personalized AI responses — thereby evaluating output quality instead of blindly copying and pasting.
Personal Knowledge Management (PKM): Understanding how to build a “Second AI Brain” to store notes, documents, and retrieve appropriate context when needed.
Soft Skills: Improved ability to ask questions, listen selectively, summarize content, and participate effectively in group discussions.
Closing Note The “Context Is Everything” workshop was a valuable experience that helped me—a student—change my approach to AI: from simply asking individual questions to thinking in terms of context-driven system design. This is a crucial foundation for my continued learning, product development, and career advancement in the field of AI.
2. Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick Suite A community session organized as part of the AWS Community Day / First Cloud Journey (FCJ) event series. The talk explored Amazon Quick Suite, a unified agentic AI experience that connects to a company’s data and world knowledge to help business users gather insights, automate tasks, and accelerate decisions.
Speakers Pham Ngoc Hai Anh – G-AsiaPacific Vietnam, AWS Community Builder. Summary of content and main activities The speaker opened by sharing how painful a typical day can be for business users: gathering and analyzing data from multiple sources, repeatedly consulting experts for specialized datasets, and repeating routine time-consuming tasks. The talk then introduced Amazon Quick Suite as AWS’s new agentic AI enterprise solution - a unified experience built around an integrated, secure view of company data that simplifies and accelerates the time from data to insight and to action.
Activity 1 — Understanding the Pain Points
Explored the daily challenges of business users: gathering information across multiple disconnected sources, relying on experts for specialized datasets, and doing the same repetitive, time-consuming tasks over and over again. Activity 2 — Introduction to Amazon Quick Suite
Learned that Quick Suite offers a unified experience built around an integrated, secure view of company data. It is AWS’s new agentic AI offering for the enterprise that simplifies and accelerates the journey from data to decision. Activity 3 — Capabilities Tour (40+ Connectors, Bedrock, Actions)
Walked through Quick Suite’s building blocks: 40+ data connectors, user-uploaded files, databases and data warehouses, Bedrock foundation models, web search, thousands of pre-built actions in third-party apps, embeddable UIs and APIs, and responsible AI features (governance, data security, access controls, guardrails, regulatory compliance). Activity 4 — Live Demo
Watched a hands-on demo of how Quick works in practice, including chat-based research, scenario analysis, dashboards, and automation flows for teams across Sales, Marketing, HR and Support. Activity 5 — Use Case: PM Assistant
Explored a concrete use case - a Project Management assistant that can automatically create Minutes of Meeting (MoM), send emails to stakeholders, and schedule the next meeting with minimal manual work. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Agentic AI is shifting from answering questions to executing end-to-end workflows - and Quick Suite is a strong example of this new direction.
A unified, secure data layer is what makes enterprise AI actually useful: without connectors, governance and access controls, even the best models cannot help the business reliably.
Automation is no longer a future idea - tools like the PM assistant show that Meetings-to-MoM-to-Email-to-Scheduling can already be handled by AI today.Context engineering will be a core skill in the future, not just prompt engineering.
AWS ecosystem (Bedrock, S3, data warehouses, third-party actions) provides a complete stack that students can absolutely learn and experiment with.
Newly Acquired Skills
Technical Knowledge: Familiarity with Amazon Quick Suite, its agentic capabilities (Chat, Research, Insights, Automation), and where Bedrock, data warehouses and third-party connectors fit in a real AI solution.
Product Thinking: Understanding how to translate a vague business pain point (e.g. repetitive reporting) into a concrete AI workflow that combines data, models and actions.
Critical Thinking: Learning to evaluate an agentic AI product beyond hype - through dimensions like governance, security, responsible AI, and integration depth.
Soft Skills: Improved active listening during demo sessions, note-taking under time pressure, asking focused questions in Q&A, and networking with community builders and peers.
Closing Note The ‘Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick’ session was a valuable experience for me as a student. It gave me a much clearer view of how agentic AI is being applied in real enterprises, and inspired me to keep learning AWS tools, data connectors and Bedrock to design useful AI assistants of my own.
3. From Edge to Origin - CloudFront as Your Foundation A foundational technical session organized as part of the AWS First Cloud AI Journey (FCAIJ) community program. The talk introduced Amazon CloudFront as the entry point for AWS networking - explaining how CloudFront works, the challenges customers face with CDN cost and security, the four key capability pillars (cost optimization, security, resiliency and performance), and the recommended use cases and best practices.
Speakers Nguyen Tuan Thinh – DevOps Engineer, First Cloud AI Journey. Summary of content and main activities The talk was framed around a powerful quote from a founder: “The worst feeling you can have as a founder is when you don’t expect a bill… then you look at the bill and think, ‘I need to fix this now.’” From that starting point, the speaker walked through the typical cost, security and performance pain points in CDN usage, then introduced how Amazon CloudFront - together with WAF, Route 53, S3 and the new Fixed-Price CDN + Security Plans (launching Nov 19, 2025) - provides a predictable, secure and high-performance foundation for every workload.
Activity 1 — Understanding the Customer Pain Points
Discussed the typical frustrations of CDN users: unpredictable bill spikes from viral traffic or DDoS attacks, difficult cost forecasting, complex monthly infrastructure calculations, and the financial risk of usage-based billing. Activity 2 — Target Personas Walkthrough
Mapped CDN needs across small website owners, business users, and scaling businesses - from basic protection to advanced features like VPC private origins, origin failover and origin load reduction. Activity 3 — Solution Overview (CDN + Security + DNS + Storage)
Walked through the AWS solution bundle: AWS WAF, Amazon S3 and Amazon Route 53 combined with CloudFront. Highlighted simple pricing, easy upgrade paths and the elimination of financial risk from unexpected spikes. Activity 4 — Fixed-Price CDN + Security Pricing Plans
Learned about the new tiered pricing plans (launching Nov 19, 2025) that bundle CDN, WAF, DDoS protection (Shield Advanced), DNS (Route 53), logging (CloudWatch) and S3 storage credits into one flat monthly price - pay the same price regardless of usage. Activity 5 — CloudFront Internals & Global Network
Explored the AWS Global Edge Network: 700+ PoPs in 50+ countries, 100+ cities, regional edge caches, redundant 400 GbE network and direct peering with major ISPs. Learned how Origin Shield centralizes caching and how request collapsing works. Activity 6 — Cost Optimization Deep Dive
Compared scenarios With vs Without CloudFront: 24-hour auto-browsing of a WordPress page on EC2 dropped from ~$8.13 to ~$2.95. EC2 origin CPU utilization fell from 5% to 1%. CloudFront HTTP compression reduced payload by 82% (44.3 kB -> 8.0 kB). Activity 7 - Security Capabilities
Hands-on tour of HTTPS in transit, Mutual TLS, Origin cloaking (VPC Origin, Origin Access Control - OAC, custom-origin firewall + secret header), geographic restrictions, and Signed URL/cookie content protection with ECDSA. Activity 8 - Resiliency & Performance Features
Discussed caching for availability, built-in failover to secondary origins, graceful failure with custom error pages, multi-layer caching (PoP -> Regional Edge Cache -> Origin Shield), HTTP/3 (QUIC/UDP) multiplexing and AWS global backbone for persistent connections. Activity 9 - Edge Computing with CloudFront Functions / Lambda@Edge
Explored running logic at the edge: URL rewrite, A/B testing, geo redirect, device-based content delivery, rate limiting, API mocking, health checks and error handling - all without reaching the origin. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
CloudFront is much more than a CDN - it is the entry point of the AWS network and works closely with WAF, Shield, Route 53 and S3 to form a complete edge stack.
Cost optimization is not just about cheaper bills - smart use of caching, compression, Origin Shield and the AWS global backbone significantly reduces load on the origin (in the demo, EC2 CPU dropped from 5% to 1%).
Security must be designed in layers: HTTPS / mTLS for transit, OAC / VPC Origin for origin cloaking, Signed URL/cookie for content protection, and geographic restrictions for audience control.
Predictable pricing matters as much as performance - the new Fixed-Price CDN + Security plans protect founders and small teams from $100K bill shock while still scaling to any workload.
Newly Acquired Skills
Technical Knowledge: Solid understanding of Amazon CloudFront architecture (PoPs, Regional Edge Caches, Origin Shield), security primitives (WAF, OAC, signed URL, mTLS), performance features (HTTP/3, compression, persistent connections) and the new tiered pricing model.
System Design Thinking: Ability to design a content-delivery architecture that balances cost, security and performance - choosing the right caching layer, origin protection strategy and failover pattern for a given application.
Observability & Cost Awareness: Practiced reading cost-comparison tables and CPU/bandwidth metrics, which helps me estimate and justify AWS spend in future student or freelance projects.
Soft Skills: Improved active listening during fast-paced technical slides, structure-taking notes on diagrams, formulating sharp technical questions during Q&A, and networking professionally with the speaker and peers after the session.
Closing Note The ‘From Edge to Origin - CloudFront as Your Foundation’ session gave me an end-to-end view of how a modern cloud network is built. From global edge PoPs to origin protection, from cost optimization to predictable pricing, every concept learned here directly supports my goal of becoming a well-rounded cloud engineer and building reliable, secure and affordable web applications.
4. 36 hrs with LotusHacks - Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality A community-session talk organized by Team VIB as part of AWS Community Day activities. The team shared their 36-hour hackathon journey at LotusHacks 2026 (Vietnam’s largest hackathon), where they built UTMorpho - from a blank page to a focused AI editing product. The session covered the team’s motivation, the ideation journey, technical execution under pressure, the finished demo, the challenges and failures they faced, and the key learnings they took home.
Speakers Team VIB – the creators and presenters of the UTMorpho project. Summary of content and main activities Team VIB opened with their motivation for joining LotusHacks: Vietnam’s largest hackathon, 36 hours, one product, learning from developers across the country, and honestly having fun. They then walked through the seven phases of their build: why they joined, going from zero to idea, defining the problem and shaping UTMorpho, building under pressure, a product overview and demo, the challenges/failures/turning points, and finally key learnings and what’s next.
Activity 1 — Why We Joined LotusHacks
Explained the team’s four motivations: Vietnam’s largest hackathon, challenge of building one product in only 36 hours, the chance to learn from developers across Vietnam and beyond, and simply having fun with peers. Activity 2 — From Zero to Idea
Walked through the very first hours: Hour 0 - signed up with an empty head, opening day - still feeling lost, the decision to go home and think quietly, and the eventual insight to look at daily work for real-world inspiration. Activity 3 — Defining the Problem & Shaping UTMorpho
Shared how the team named the real problem they were facing, then formulated it as UTMorpho. The product started not as a ‘big idea’ but as a focused answer to a frustration they actually had in everyday work. Activity 4 - Building Under Pressure (36-hr Timeline)
Broke the sprint into five clear phases: Setup & Alignment -> First Slice -> Build the Core -> The Hard Middle (Integration & Polish) -> Submit & Pitch. This showed how they managed energy, scope and quality inside the 36-hour window. Activity 5 — UTMorpho Product Overview & Demo
Demoed the finished product with a focused editing experience. Showed how UTMorpho helps users shape content in a clean, distraction-free interface - the antithesis of the ‘AI overgeneration’ problem the team themselves felt. Activity 6 — Challenges, Failures & Turning Points
Openly listed the three biggest issues: AI overgeneration (too much output, not useful), burnout as the pitch time approached, and hitting token limits. Turning those pains into product decisions was the key moment of the hackathon. Activity 7 - The Big Reframe (Focused Editing Experience)
Explained how the team chose to build a focused editing experience rather than another ‘AI writes everything for you’ tool - a deliberate design philosophy that came directly from their own frustration. Activity 8 - Key Takeaways
Two simple but powerful lessons: ‘Real frustration creates real ideas’ and ‘More ideas is not equal to better ideas’ - quality of problem selection matters more than volume of brainstormed features. Activity 9 - What Hackathons Really Test
Reflected on what the sprint really measured: not coding speed, but endurance, and not just skill, but team synchronization - the ability to align, support each other and ship together under fatigue. Activity 10 - What’s Next
Outlined three next steps: continue building the UTMorpho idea, integrate it into real workflows (not just a hackathon demo), and share the hackathon journey with the wider student community. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Hackathons test endurance and team sync far more than raw coding speed - shipping matters more than perfection.
The best product ideas come from real, personal pain points - not from trying to brainstorm ‘big’ ideas.
More features is not better - one well-defined problem with a focused solution wins over a broad product that does many things poorly.
When working under time pressure, breaking the work into clear phases (Setup -> First Slice -> Core -> Hard Middle -> Submit) keeps the team aligned and reduces panic near the deadline.
Newly Acquired Skills
Product & Design Thinking: Learned that identifying a real frustration, naming the problem clearly, and building a focused experience is often more impactful than adding more AI features.
Hackathon Strategy: Understanding how to split a 36-hour sprint into phases, manage scope, handle burnout and pace the team toward a confident pitch.
AI Tool Awareness: Recognized common AI pitfalls (overgeneration, token limits) and how product design choices can turn those pitfalls into product strengths.
Soft Skills: Improved active listening on a fast-paced storytelling deck, structured note-taking on timelines and turning points, asking focused follow-up questions, and connecting with other students who share hackathon interests.
Closing Note The Team VIB / UTMorpho session was an inspiring reminder that great products can come out of 36 hours of focused, painful, real work. As a student I walked away with a clearer mindset for the next hackathon I join: start from real frustration, build with a small focused team, structure the sprint in phases, and ship something honest rather than something ‘big’.
5. Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings A deep-dive technical talk based on the research paper by Atil et al. (2024) - Penn State University & Comcast AI Technologies. The session demystified why LLMs - even when configured with temperature = 0 - still produce non-deterministic outputs, and showed students how to mitigate that reality in production-style AI projects.
Speakers Duc Dao – Solution Architect at Cloud Kinetics. Summary of content and main activities The talk was structured around four milestones: (1) how LLMs choose the next token, (2) the common assumption about deterministic settings, (3) the technical reality revealed by the paper, and (4) practical mitigation strategies. The core message: temperature=0 is often treated as a reliability guarantee, but in practice it does not fully remove variance.
Activity 1 — How LLMs Actually Choose the Next Token
Walked through the generation loop step by step: the model produces a score (logit) for every word in its vocabulary, softmax converts those scores into probabilities, the next token is sampled from that distribution, and the loop repeats until the response ends. Activity 2 — The Role of Temperature (T)
Explained T > 1 (more randomness), T = 1 (natural variation), T approaching 0 (consistent / ‘confident’), and T = 0 which theoretically always picks the single highest-probability token (argmax) - the foundation of ‘deterministic’ behaviour. Activity 3 — Visualizing Temperature, Top-P and Top-K
Visual demo showing how temperature, nucleus sampling (Top-P) and Top-K reshape the probability distribution and therefore the diversity of outputs. Activity 4 - The Assumption: temp=0 = Deterministic
Mapped the four production use cases that rely on this assumption: structured outputs (JSON/YAML/XML), production reliability, regression testing, prompt A/B evaluation and LLM benchmarking. Activity 5 — The Reality: What the Researchers Did
Reviewed the experimental setup: 5 LLMs (GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B, Llama-3-8B, Mixtral-8x7B), 8 NLP benchmark tasks, 10 runs per condition with identical prompt and identical temp=0 settings, both zero-shot and few-shot, plus new metrics TARr@N and TARa@N. Activity 6 — The Reality: What They Found
Highlighted the shocking results: zero models were consistent; accuracy varied up to 15% across identical runs; best-to-worst accuracy gap reached 70% on the same task; TARr@10 (exact string match) was often near 0% on harder tasks. Activity 7 - Technical Root Cause: Floating-Point Non-Associativity
Explained that IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic is non-associative on GPUs: (a + b) + c is not always equal to a + (b + c). Parallel GPU kernels run operations in non-deterministic order, so tiny rounding differences in logits can flip the argmax. Activity 8 - Commercial Root Cause: Inference Batching
Discussed how API providers batch multiple users’ requests together for GPU efficiency, so a single request’s computation changes depending on what else is in the same batch - a fundamental tradeoff of cost-efficient inference. Activity 9 - Short Live Demo
Watched a short in-session demo that reproduced non-determinism with temp=0, making the abstract paper result tangible for students. Activity 10 - Why This Matters in High-Stakes Domains
Connected the issue to medical information retrieval, legal research, financial planning and analytics - any high-stakes production app where variance is unacceptable and ’temp=0’ is wrongly trusted as a guarantee. Activity 11 - Mitigation Strategies
Reviewed five practical strategies: multiple runs + majority vote (’ensemble’), structured/constrained outputs (JSON mode, function calling, regex grammars), self-hosted models with deterministic infrastructure, designing for variance from the start, and thorough testing. Activity 12 - Practical Tips (temp=0 vs temp=0.1)
Shared hands-on tips: temp=0 triggers greedy decoding and can cause repetitive loops; temp=0.1 is often a ‘sweet spot’ that is still nearly deterministic; adjusting repeat penalty helps reduce looping (with caution for code generation). Final reminder: always Read the Docs. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Temperature = 0 is not a true reproducibility guarantee - it only removes sampling randomness, not the non-determinism from GPU floating-point math or inference batching.
Non-determinism is a property of the stack (GPU, FP precision, inference optimizer), not a property of the prompt or even of the model alone.
Structured outputs (JSON mode, function calling, regex grammars) are often the cheapest and most robust way to reduce variance for production work.
Any AI feature with real consequences should be designed to tolerate variance from day one - never assume ‘same prompt = same answer’.
temp=0.1 is often more reliable than temp=0 in practice, because it avoids greedy-decoding loops while still feeling nearly deterministic.
Newly Acquired Skills
Technical / AI Engineering: Solid mental model of token sampling (logits -> softmax -> argmax vs sampling), the role of Temperature / Top-P / Top-K, and why GPU floating-point arithmetic is non-associative in IEEE 754.
LLM Engineering Best Practices: Confidence in choosing sampling parameters, using constrained/structured outputs, applying ensemble/majority-vote when correctness matters, and testing for variance instead of testing for a single ‘correct’ answer.
Research Literacy: Ability to read and translate a research paper into actionable engineering rules - useful for class projects that cite academic references.
Soft Skills: Improved listening on a dense technical topic, taking structured notes on formulas and metrics, formulating precise follow-up questions, and discussing technical tradeoffs with peers.
Closing Note The ‘Non-Determinism of Deterministic LLM Settings’ session reshaped how I think about LLM outputs - they are inherently probabilistic, and treating them as deterministic sources is a recipe for silent bugs. As a student I walked away with stronger AI engineering habits (constrained outputs, ensembles, variance-aware design) that I will carry into my next coursework and side projects.
6. Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System - The Case of Startup Credit Scoring A flagship session at AWS Vietnam Community Day 2025 (edition 5 - 2026 schedule, executive-program-style session). The talk walked through how to design, build and operate an enterprise-grade multi-agent AI system for credit scoring - using a startup lending use case as the real-world anchor.
Speakers Vy Lam – Senior Business Systems Analyst at VPBank (one of Vietnam’s largest banks). Summary of content and main activities Vy Lam framed the session around a simple but painful truth: traditional credit scoring was designed for established businesses with 3+ years of financials, collateral and predictable revenue - none of which describe a 6-18 month-old startup. The talk then showed how a virtual credit committee built from specialized AI agents can solve this gap, and walked through the guardrails, AWS deployment path and ROI required to take such a system from proof-of-concept to production.
Activity 1 — The Structural Mismatch
Compared traditional banking requirements (3+ years of financials, credit history, collateral, predictable revenue, industry benchmarks, standardized reporting) with the reality of startups (6-18 months of operations, no credit history, IP/team/traction as assets, hypergrowth or pivot patterns, novel business models, unstructured data everywhere). Insight: ‘startups are rejected not because they are bad investments, but because they do not fit the data model.’ Activity 2 — Startup Data is Multi-Dimensional
Mapped the four analytical dimensions: financial (cash flow, burn, economics, cap table), market (TAM/SAM/SOM, competition, timing, regulation), team (founders, composition, advisors, track record), and traction (user growth, engagement, retention, customer validation). Lesson: a single model cannot master all domains effectively. Activity 3 — When Single Agent Is and Is Not Appropriate
Reviewed decision criteria. Single agent is good for single-domain expertise, linear workflows, simple tool orchestration, low-stakes decisions and rapid prototyping. It is not appropriate when multi-domain expertise is required, decisions are complex/tree-structured, objectives conflict, auditability matters, or the system is enterprise-grade. Activity 4 - The Single Agent Problem
Walked through the failure mode: a single agent consumes all input data (financials, team, metadata) and produces an unreliable final credit decision. Critical limitations highlighted: context limits, expertise dilution, no checks & balances and a single point of failure. Activity 5 — Virtual Credit Committee (Multi-Agent Blueprint)
Introduced the multi-agent architecture: a Manager orchestrates five specialized agents - Financial Analyst (cash flow, burn, economics), Market Analyst (TAM, competition, timing), Team Evaluator (founders, track record), Risk Assessor (downside, mitigation) and Compliance (regulatory, policy). Together they emit a consensus output: Credit Score (0-100), Risk Rating (A-F), Confidence (High/Med/Low) and a full Audit Trail. Activity 6 - Why Multi-Agent Architecture Works
Six reasons: specialized expertise, checks & balances, parallel processing, auditability, scalability (add agents without retraining) and fault tolerance (one failure does not crash the pipeline). Activity 7 - Six Pillars of Enterprise-Grade AI
Mapped the enterprise mindset across security (auth, encryption, secrets), data governance (residency, PII, audit log), network (VPC, private endpoints, zero-trust), operations (monitoring, IR, DR), human factors (training, runbooks, knowledge transfer) and compliance (regulations, internal policies, certifications). Activity 8 - Guardrails Framework
Detailed three layers of guardrails: input (content filtering, PII detection, injection prevention, rate limiting), processing (token budgets, timeout, model selection, tool access controls) and output (response validation, hallucination detection, bias checking, compliance verification). Mapped to standards: SOC 2, GDPR, PCI DSS and banking regulations. Activity 9 - All Aspects of Application Security
Looked at security across perimeter (WAF, DDoS, rate limiting), network (VPC, Security Groups, PrivateLink), identity (IAM, Cognito, RBAC, MFA), application (input validation, prompt-injection prevention), data (encryption at rest and in transit, tokenization) and AI-specific controls (prompt injection, output filtering, key rotation, immutable audit trail). Activity 10 - Proposed Deployment Approach (AgentCore)
Walked through the six-step deployment path: Local app (CrewAI) -> Amazon Bedrock AgentCore runtime -> Docker container -> ECR registry -> Bedrock service -> API Gateway endpoint, with enterprise concerns: VPC isolation, IAM boundaries, AWS Secrets Manager, monitoring (CloudWatch, X-Ray, OpenTelemetry), auto-scaling and DR strategy. Activity 11 - ROI Analysis
Compared traditional credit-scoring process (2-3 weeks, 40-60 analyst hours, 15-20% approval rate, ~100M VND per decision) with multi-agent (2-4 hours, 95% faster, 2-4 analyst hours, 35-45% approval, <5M VND per decision). Concluded with: VND 2-3B annual savings, 12-15 month payback, 3-year ROI 200%-300%. Activity 12 - Implementation Roadmap (4 Phases)
Phase 1 Foundation (env, core agent, guardrails, internal testing), Phase 2 Integration (data, security, API, UAT), Phase 3 Pilot (limited prod, performance, feedback loop, compliance audit), Phase 4 Scale (full prod, monitoring, training, optimization). Activity 13 - Workshop Exercises
Five hands-on exercises: add Authentication (Cognito/OAuth2 + JWT), implement Bedrock Guardrails, set IAM permission boundaries, integrate Model Context Protocol (MCP) for tool integration, and write Terraform for reproducible deployment. Resources shared: awslabs repo, step-by-step docs, Terraform modules, reference architecture. Activity 14 - Key Takeaways & Final Punch Line
Four takeaways: (1) architecture matters, multi-agent gives specialized expertise and auditability, (2) enterprise-grade is a mindset from day one, (3) AWS provides the building blocks (AgentCore, ECR, API Gateway), (4) ROI is compelling. Closing line: ‘Enterprise AI is not about making things work - it is about making them work securely, reliably and at scale’. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Real-world problems rarely fit a single model - specialized agents that collaborate on a shared decision usually win over one big monolithic prompt.
Enterprise-grade is not a final layer to bolt on - security, governance, compliance and operations must be baked in from day one.
Auditability is the soul of regulated AI - a system you cannot explain is a system you cannot ship in banking.
Numbers sell ideas - showing 95% faster processing and 200%-300% ROI is what gets executive buy-in for AI projects in traditional industries.
AWS building blocks (Bedrock, AgentCore, ECR, API Gateway, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch, X-Ray) already cover most enterprise requirements - students should learn them on AWS Free Tier before joining real teams.
Newly Acquired Skills
Multi-Agent System Design: Mental model for designing a manager-orchestrated agent team with domain specialists, parallel processing and a consensus output - the blueprint behind modern agentic AI products.
System Architecture & Decision Trees: Ability to choose single-agent vs multi-agent based on domain count, decision complexity, risk level and auditability - useful for any AI capstone or product decision.
Enterprise Security & Compliance: Familiarity with WAF + DDoS + IAM + Cognito + VPC + PrivateLink + Guardrails, SOC 2 / GDPR / PCI DSS, PII handling and immutable audit trails.
AWS Cloud / DevOps: Hands-on mental model of CrewAI -> AgentCore -> Docker -> ECR -> Bedrock -> API Gateway deployment with Terraform, CloudWatch, X-Ray and OpenTelemetry.
Soft Skills: Sharpened listening during architecture-heavy presentations, structured note-taking on diagrams and ROI tables, and asking pragmatic questions about cost/credits/pricing.
Closing Note Vy Lam’s talk was a clear reminder that real enterprise AI is far beyond ‘make the chatbot work’. It is about specialized agents that collaborate, secure pipelines, auditable outputs and a business case that finance teams can sign off on. As a student I walked away with a sharper architectural vocabulary, more confidence in agentic AI design, and a concrete checklist for taking future student or capstone projects from POC to ‘production-shaped’.

title: Create DynamoDB, S3, and test the API
content:
Create DynamoDB tables Create the base journal table first:
Table name: JournalLogs Partition key: userId Sort key: entryId Table settings: Default settings Then create AI-related tables that will be used in the next section:
Table name: AiReports Partition key: userId Sort key: reportId Table name: Quizzes Partition key: userId Sort key: quizId Table name: QuizAttempts Partition key: userId Sort key: attemptId Grant Lambda data permissions Open the execution role of AI-Personal-Learning-Handle and attach permissions for:
DynamoDB read and write access to the four tables. S3 read and write access to the image bucket. SQS send-message access to the AI queues. CloudWatch Logs permissions for debugging. For a short workshop, managed policies can speed up the build. For production, replace them with resource-scoped policies.
Create the S3 image bucket Open Amazon S3 and choose Create bucket. Bucket name: learnflow-journal-images-<unique-suffix>. Region: same Region as the Lambda API. Keep Block Public Access enabled. Create the bucket. Configure bucket CORS so the frontend can upload through presigned URLs:
[ { "AllowedHeaders": ["*"], "AllowedMethods": ["GET", "PUT", "POST"], "AllowedOrigins": ["http://localhost:5173"], "ExposeHeaders": ["ETag"], "MaxAgeSeconds": 3000 } ] Add routes for journal and image upload In API Gateway, create and attach the Lambda integration for:
GET /logs POST /logs GET /logs/{id} PUT /logs/{id} DELETE /logs/{id} POST /logs/{id}/images/presigned-url Attach CognitoAuth to every route above.
Test end-to-end Call GET /health from the browser. A successful response returns JSON similar to {"ok":true}. Sign in through Cognito Hosted UI. Call GET /me with the JWT token and verify that the API returns user information. Create a journal log through POST /logs. Request a presigned URL, upload an image to S3, and save the image key in the journal log. Reload the frontend and verify that journal data and images are loaded for the signed-in user only.

title: Create the Lambda AI Worker
content:
In this section, you will create and configure the Lambda worker that processes AI jobs from SQS.
Create the worker function Open AWS Lambda and choose Create function. Function name: AI-Personal-Learning-Worker. Runtime: Node.js 22.x. Choose an execution role with permissions for DynamoDB, SQS, and CloudWatch. Create the function. Configure timeout and concurrency AI calls can take longer than the default Lambda timeout. Configure:
Timeout: 30 seconds or more Reserved concurrency: a small value such as 2–5 during the workshop Reserved concurrency helps prevent accidental high parallel AI usage and keeps cost predictable.
Store the Groq API key securely In Configuration > Environment variables, add:
GROQ_API_KEY=<your-groq-api-key> GROQ_MODEL=llama-3.1-8b-instant In code, read the key from process.env.GROQ_API_KEY. Avoid committing the key to GitHub or pasting it into workshop reports.
Worker code Use the following complete worker code for index.mjs:
import { DynamoDBClient } from "@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb"; import { DynamoDBDocumentClient, QueryCommand, UpdateCommand, } from "@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb"; const REGION = process.env.AWS_REGION || "ap-southeast-1"; const JOURNAL_TABLE = process.env.JOURNAL_TABLE || "JournalLogs"; const AI_REPORT_TABLE = process.env.AI_REPORT_TABLE || "AiReports"; const QUIZ_TABLE = process.env.QUIZ_TABLE || "Quizzes"; const GROQ_API_KEY = process.env.GROQ_API_KEY; const GROQ_MODEL = process.env.GROQ_MODEL || "llama-3.1-8b-instant"; const docClient = DynamoDBDocumentClient.from( new DynamoDBClient({ region: REGION }), ); async function callGroqAI(prompt) { if (!GROQ_API_KEY) { throw new Error("Missing GROQ_API_KEY environment variable"); } const response = await fetch( "https://api.groq.com/openai/v1/chat/completions", { method: "POST", headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${GROQ_API_KEY}`, "Content-Type": "application/json", }, body: JSON.stringify({ model: GROQ_MODEL, messages: [ { role: "system", content: "You are a learning assistant. Return valid JSON only. Do not wrap JSON in markdown fences.", }, { role: "user", content: prompt }, ], temperature: 0.3, }), }, ); if (!response.ok) { const errorText = await response.text(); throw new Error(`Groq API Error ${response.status}: ${errorText}`); } const data = await response.json(); return data.choices?.[0]?.message?.content || "{}"; } async function getUserLogs(userId, startDate, endDate, tags = []) { const result = await docClient.send( new QueryCommand({ TableName: JOURNAL_TABLE, KeyConditionExpression: "userId = :uid", ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":uid": userId }, }), ); return (result.Items || []).filter((item) => { const inDateRange = (!startDate || item.date >= startDate) && (!endDate || item.date <= endDate); const hasTags = tags.length === 0 || tags.some((tag) => (item.tags || []) .map((value) => String(value).toLowerCase()) .includes(String(tag).toLowerCase()), ); return inDateRange && hasTags; }); } function parseJson(text) { const clean = text .replace(/```json/g, "") .replace(/```/g, "") .trim(); return JSON.parse(clean); } async function handleReportJob(message) { const { userId, reportId, type, startDate, endDate, tags = [] } = message; await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: AI_REPORT_TABLE, Key: { userId, reportId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "processing", ":updatedAt": new Date().toISOString(), }, }), ); const logs = await getUserLogs(userId, startDate, endDate, tags); const logsText = logs .map( (log) => `Date: ${log.date}\nTitle: ${log.title}\nContent: ${log.content}`, ) .join("\n\n"); const prompt = `Analyze these learning journal entries and return JSON: { "summary": "short learning progress summary", "strengths": ["strength 1", "strength 2"], "weaknesses": ["weakness 1", "weakness 2"], "recommendations": ["recommendation 1", "recommendation 2"] } Report type: ${type || "weekly"} Journal entries: ${logsText || "No journal entries found."}`; const aiResult = parseJson(await callGroqAI(prompt)); await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: AI_REPORT_TABLE, Key: { userId, reportId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, summary = :summary, strengths = :strengths, weaknesses = :weaknesses, recommendations = :recommendations, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "completed", ":summary": aiResult.summary || "", ":strengths": aiResult.strengths || [], ":weaknesses": aiResult.weaknesses || [], ":recommendations": aiResult.recommendations || [], ":updatedAt": new Date().toISOString(), }, }), ); } async function handleQuizJob(message) { const { userId, quizId, topic, questionCount = 5, difficulty = "medium", startDate, endDate, tags = [], } = message; await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: QUIZ_TABLE, Key: { userId, quizId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "processing", ":updatedAt": new Date().toISOString(), }, }), ); const logs = await getUserLogs(userId, startDate, endDate, tags); const logsText = logs .map((log) => `${log.title}: ${log.content}`) .join("\n\n"); const prompt = `Create ${questionCount} multiple-choice questions for topic "${topic}". Return JSON only: { "questions": [ { "topic": "${topic}", "prompt": "question text", "options": ["A", "B", "C", "D"], "answer_index": 0, "explanation": "why this answer is correct" } ] } Difficulty: ${difficulty} Learning notes: ${logsText || topic}`; const aiResult = parseJson(await callGroqAI(prompt)); await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: QUIZ_TABLE, Key: { userId, quizId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, questions = :questions, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "completed", ":questions": aiResult.questions || [], ":updatedAt": new Date().toISOString(), }, }), ); } async function markFailed(message, error) { const now = new Date().toISOString(); if (message.reportId) { await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: AI_REPORT_TABLE, Key: { userId: message.userId, reportId: message.reportId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, errorMessage = :error, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "failed", ":error": error.message, ":updatedAt": now, }, }), ); } if (message.quizId) { await docClient.send( new UpdateCommand({ TableName: QUIZ_TABLE, Key: { userId: message.userId, quizId: message.quizId }, UpdateExpression: "SET #status = :status, errorMessage = :error, updatedAt = :updatedAt", ExpressionAttributeNames: { "#status": "status" }, ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":status": "failed", ":error": error.message, ":updatedAt": now, }, }), ); } } export const handler = async (event) => { for (const record of event.Records || []) { const message = JSON.parse(record.body || "{}"); try { if (message.reportId) { await handleReportJob(message); } else if (message.quizId) { await handleQuizJob(message); } else { throw new Error("Unknown AI job type"); } } catch (error) { console.error("Lambda AI Worker error:", error); await markFailed(message, error); throw error; } } return { ok: true }; }; Add SQS triggers In the Lambda function designer, choose Add trigger. Source: SQS. Queue: ai-report-jobs. Add the trigger. Repeat for quiz-jobs. Worker behavior The worker should:
Parse each SQS message. Mark the target report or quiz as processing. Query JournalLogs for the current user and selected time range, tags, or topic. Build a prompt for Groq. Save the AI response to DynamoDB. Mark the item as completed, or failed if an exception occurs. Reference screenshots

title: Prerequiste
content:
Required accounts and tools Prepare the following before starting:
An AWS account with permission to create Cognito, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, SQS, CloudWatch, SNS, Budgets, Amplify, CloudFront, and WAF resources. A frontend repository, for example a React/Vite SPA, pushed to GitHub so Amplify can deploy it. A Google Cloud project if you want Cognito Hosted UI to support Google sign-in. A Groq account and API key for AI model completion. A local development URL such as http://localhost:5173 for frontend testing. Recommended naming convention Use consistent names to keep the workshop easier to follow:
Cognito user pool: AI-Personal-Learning-UserPool Main Lambda API: AI-Personal-Learning-Handle Lambda AI Worker: AI-Personal-Learning-Worker HTTP API: AI-Personal-Learning-API S3 bucket: learnflow-journal-images-<unique-suffix> DynamoDB tables: JournalLogs, AiReports, Quizzes, QuizAttempts SQS queues: ai-report-jobs, quiz-jobs, and DLQs such as ai-report-dlq and quiz-dlq Security preparation Never hardcode the Groq API key in source code. Store it in Lambda environment variables or AWS Secrets Manager. Use Cognito JWT authorizers for all user-specific API routes. Keep S3 Block Public Access enabled and expose images only through presigned URLs. Start with least-privilege IAM policies where possible. Managed full-access policies are acceptable for a short workshop demo, but should be narrowed before production. Region Use a single AWS Region consistently. In the sample workshop, the default region is ap-southeast-1 (Singapore) for Lambda, S3, SQS, and DynamoDB.

title: Proposal
content:
AI Personal Learning A Serverless AWS Platform for Reflective Learning and AI-Assisted Progress Review 1. Executive Summary The project involves building a web platform that helps learners keep a daily learning journal, including text and attached images. AI is then used to analyze, synthesize, and generate weekly/monthly reports on learning progress, strengths and weaknesses, study habits, and suggestions for improvement. It also includes creating review quizzes, searching, and tracking progress.
The entire system is deployed on AWS using a serverless architecture. The frontend is deployed using AWS Amplify Hosting, distributed via Amazon CloudFront, and protected by AWS WAF. User authentication is done using Amazon Cognito. The backend utilizes Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon S3, Amazon SQS, DLQ, Amazon CloudWatch, Amazon SNS, AWS Budgets, and Groq API for AI processing.
The goal is to create a useful learning support product that demonstrates the ability to design a modern, secure, cost-effective cloud architecture, handle asynchronous operations, and is easy to operate for small teams.
2. Project Objectives Main Objectives: 2.1. Support for Self-Reflection Learning Users can keep a learning journal (text + images), which is analyzed and summarized by AI into weekly/monthly reports, helping them review their learning journey in a structured way.
2.2. Personalized Learning Suggestions AI uses journal content and progress to suggest: which lessons to focus on, which skills, and which habits need adjustment.
2.3. Serverless Architecture Practice on AWS Applying serverless, event-driven, and AI (Groq) architecture in a real-world system, meeting the principles of AWS Well Architected (cost, security, availability, operation).
2.4. Cost Optimization for Small Teams Designing infrastructure to run at low cost: using Lambda, S3, DynamoDB/SQS, pay-per-use, and Monitoring & Cost Guardrails (CloudWatch + Budgets).
3. Problem Statement Current Situation Learning notes are often scattered across Notion, Google Docs, screenshots, chat messages, and AI conversations. This makes it difficult for learners to review their progress over time. Mentors also need to read many separate logs if they want to understand a learner’s development, repeated mistakes, and improvement trend.
General AI chat tools can summarize text, but they usually do not understand the learner’s structured timeline, tags, tasks, categories, or historical progress. As a result, feedback can become generic and hard to turn into concrete actions.
Proposed Solution The platform centralizes learning records in one application. Each journal entry contains structured fields such as title, content, date, category, tags, commands, errors, solutions, mood, difficulty, study time, and image attachments.
AI features are added on top of this structured data:
Weekly or monthly learning reports. Strengths, weaknesses, and recommendations. Quiz generation for review. Search across personal learning logs. Analytics summary by category, tag, mood, difficulty, and study time. Long-running AI tasks are processed asynchronously through SQS and a Lambda AI Worker, so the frontend remains responsive and users can poll for completion.
4. Solution Architecture Edge and Frontend Layer AWS Amplify Hosting deploys the React/Vite SPA directly from GitHub. Amazon CloudFront delivers static assets globally over HTTPS. AWS WAF protects the public edge with managed rules and rate-based controls. Authentication Layer Amazon Cognito User Pool handles sign-up, sign-in, and optional Google federation. The SPA receives JWT tokens from Cognito. API Gateway JWT Authorizer protects user-specific API routes. Application Layer Amazon API Gateway HTTP API exposes routes such as /health, /me, /logs, /ai/reports, /quiz, /search, and /analytics/summary. Lambda API (AI-Personal-Learning-Handle) handles synchronous requests, journal CRUD, image presigned URL generation, and SQS job submission. Lambda Search Handler supports keyword, topic, tag, and date filtering. Lambda Analytics Handler aggregates journal data into dashboard metrics. Data and File Layer DynamoDB stores journal logs, AI reports, quizzes, and quiz attempts. S3 stores user-uploaded learning images. Images are uploaded through presigned URLs so they do not pass through Lambda or API Gateway. AI Processing Layer SQS stores AI report and quiz jobs. DLQ stores failed messages for troubleshooting. Lambda AI Worker (AI-Personal-Learning-Worker) reads jobs from SQS, loads relevant journal data from DynamoDB, calls Groq API, and saves AI results back to DynamoDB. Monitoring and Cost Guardrails CloudWatch Logs, Metrics, and Alarms monitor Lambda errors, duration, throttles, and SQS backlog. SNS sends alert notifications. AWS Budgets alerts when spending reaches configured thresholds. API Gateway throttling and Lambda reserved concurrency limit accidental high usage. 5. Technical Implementation Main Features Cognito registration, login, logout, and Google sign-in. Journal CRUD with structured learning fields. S3 image upload using presigned URLs. AI report generation through SQS and Lambda Worker. Quiz generation and quiz attempt scoring. Search endpoint for journal discovery. Analytics endpoint for progress dashboard. Amplify deployment with environment variables. WAF, throttling, CloudWatch alarms, SNS, and Budgets. Core AWS Services Amplify Hosting: frontend deployment. CloudFront + WAF: public delivery and edge protection. Cognito: authentication and JWT tokens. API Gateway: HTTP API entry point. Lambda: API handler, AI worker, search handler, analytics handler. DynamoDB: serverless NoSQL data store. S3: image storage. SQS + DLQ: asynchronous AI job processing. CloudWatch + SNS + Budgets: monitoring and cost control. Groq API: AI completion for reports and quizzes. 6. Timeline and Milestones Phase 1 - Analysis and Architecture Define user flows for journal, image upload, AI report, quiz, search, and analytics. Design DynamoDB tables and API routes. Finalize AWS serverless architecture and cost guardrails. Phase 2 - Frontend and Authentication Build the SPA screens. Configure Cognito User Pool, Hosted UI, app client, callback URLs, and Google provider. Connect frontend authentication state to API calls. Phase 3 - Backend API and Storage Create API Gateway and Lambda API. Implement journal CRUD. Create DynamoDB tables. Create S3 image bucket and presigned URL upload flow. Phase 4 - AI Pipeline Create SQS queues and DLQs. Add AI report and quiz routes. Create Lambda AI Worker and integrate Groq API. Store AI outputs in DynamoDB and support frontend polling. Phase 5 - Search, Analytics, and Deployment Create search and analytics Lambda handlers. Deploy the frontend with Amplify. Configure environment variables and Cognito production callback URLs. Phase 6 - Monitoring, Cost, and Handover Configure CloudWatch alarms, SNS notifications, API throttling, Lambda timeouts, and AWS Budgets. Prepare demo data and documentation. Clean up or hand over resources. 7. Budget Estimation For a small internship/demo environment, the expected cost is low because most services are serverless and pay-per-use.
Lambda, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB, S3, CloudWatch, SNS, and Amplify can remain within a few USD per month for light traffic. Groq API usage depends on the number of AI reports and quizzes generated. The recommended demo budget is USD 5-10/month, with AWS Budgets alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100%. Cost controls include:
API Gateway throttling on AI routes. Reserved concurrency for the AI Worker. SQS DLQ to avoid infinite retry loops. S3 lifecycle rules for uploaded images. CloudWatch alarms for error rate and queue backlog. 8. Risk Assessment Cost Overrun AI generation and repeated API calls can increase cost. Mitigation: throttling, concurrency limits, Budgets alerts, and clear frontend retry behavior.
Sensitive Learning Data Journal logs may contain personal notes or screenshots. Mitigation: Cognito authentication, JWT authorizer, private S3 bucket, presigned URLs, and least-privilege IAM.
AI Output Quality AI reports or quizzes may be incomplete or inaccurate. Mitigation: structured prompts, JSON-only output, validation in Lambda Worker, and user feedback.
Operational Complexity Multiple serverless components can be hard to troubleshoot. Mitigation: CloudWatch logs, DLQ inspection, clear naming conventions, and documentation.
9. Expected Outcomes A working AI-assisted learning journal application. Secure authentication and per-user data isolation. Fast frontend experience through Amplify and CloudFront. Scalable backend using API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, and SQS. AI reports and quizzes generated asynchronously through Groq. Monitoring and cost guardrails suitable for an internship/demo environment.

title: Week 10 Worklog
content:
Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date 1 Complete Lab41: - Studied AWS IAM Federation, SAML, and Single Sign-On (SSO) concepts. - Created and configured an Azure Active Directory tenant and users. - Applied IP address and time-based conditions to IAM roles. - Verified role access restrictions and summarized IAM best practices. 25/06/2026 25/06/2026 5 Complete Lab47: - Studied AWS Step Functions concepts and workflow orchestration. - Learned State Machine architecture and business workflow. - Created a workflow using Task, Choice, and Parallel states. - Integrated AWS Lambda functions into the state machine. - Tested workflow execution using the Step Functions console. 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 6 Continue with Lab47: - Configured Wait for Task Token and callback pattern. - Implemented Pause/Resume workflow for manual approval. - Added Retry and Catch mechanisms for error handling. - Tested different workflow scenarios and exception handling. - Monitored execution history and debugging information. 27/06/2026 27/06/2026 7 Complete Lab47: - Studied AWS Elastic Beanstalk and CloudFormation concepts. - Created a CloudFormation stack and deployed AWS infrastructure. - Configured AWS CLI, key pair, and IAM permissions. - Connected to the Windows EC2 instance and configured the MySQL database. - Imported the TravelBuddy project into Eclipse and configured the application. 28/06/2026 28/06/2026 Week 10 Achievements: Lab41:
Successfully integrated Azure AD with AWS IAM Federation using SAML. Enabled Single Sign-On (SSO) for Azure AD users into AWS. Established role-based access control across AWS services (S3, EC2). Automated synchronization of AWS IAM roles into Azure AD. Validated secure federated login and permissions enforcement. Gained clear understanding of AWS cost optimization models: Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, and Reserved DB Instances. Practiced purchasing and managing Savings Plans including scheduling and deletion of queued commitments. Learned differences between Standard vs. Convertible RIs, and how marketplace resale works. Understood how Reserved DB Instances provide discounts while requiring strict configuration alignment. Showcased how IAM federation and conditional policies enhance security posture while maintaining flexibility. Lab47:
Successfully set up Cloud9 + SAM environment for serverless development. Built and tested Lambda-based services for account applications and data validation.
