title: Event 3
content:
Summary Report: “AWS FIRST CLOUD AI JOURNEY COMMUNITY DAY 30/05/2026” 1. First Cloud AI Journey - Level Up Your AWS Skills with Cloud Quest and Floci A community-driven, hands-on technical meetup focused on helping beginners - especially students and junior developers - start their AWS journey without the fear of large bills. The session walked through two practical tools (AWS Cloud Quest and open-source Floci) and showed how combining them creates a complete, low-cost learning roadmap for real cloud skills.
Speakers Huynh Thai Linh – AWS community learner and presenter, sharing a personal AWS Summary of content and main activities Huynh Thai Linh opened the session with a very relatable pain point: most beginners who try to learn AWS hands-on end up stressed about checking the billing dashboard every hour, forgetting to delete resources and accumulating surprise charges. The talk then reframed this problem by introducing a complete, zero-cost (or near-zero-cost) learning path built on two complementary tools: AWS Cloud Quest for guided, game-based architecture practice, and open-source Floci for lightning-fast local AWS emulation. The session finished with a clear 3-phase roadmap and an honest discussion of where each tool falls short.
Activity 1 — What Beginners Worry About in AWS
Framed the core problem: students constantly check the AWS bill, worry about forgetting to delete resources (EC2, RDS, NAT Gateway, Elastic IPs), and fear extra charges from idle services. Established why beginners avoid hands-on labs - and why that blocks real learning. Activity 2 — The Three AWS Learning Tool Categories
Mapped the three ways to practice AWS today: (a) real AWS account - high realism but high cost; (b) commercial emulators like LocalStack Pro - paid, sometimes slow; (c) free open-source emulators - cost-free but with feature limits. Set up the trade-off space. Activity 3 — AWS Cloud Quest: A Free Hands-on Learning Playground
Introduced AWS Cloud Quest as a free, game-based, guided learning environment. Highlights: no credit card billing stress, structured quests for beginners, focused on building the AWS architecture mindset rather than memorising service lists. Activity 4 — Why Cloud Quest Works for Beginners
Three reasons: (1) guided AWS practice - no need to design labs from scratch; (2) easy entry for beginners with zero AWS background; (3) learning through game quests keeps motivation high. Positioned Cloud Quest as the ‘mindset & architecture’ phase. Activity 5 — Open-Source Floci: Local AWS Testing
Introduced Floci as an open-source AWS service emulator that runs entirely on the local machine. Key benefits: AWS service emulation, zero cloud costs, fast architecture testing during development, ideal for coding and CI pipelines. Activity 6 - Floci vs. LocalStack: Performance & Coverage
Presented the head-to-head benchmark. Floci is up to 138x faster than LocalStack on common operations, uses up to 11x less memory and storage, and supports more free services than the LocalStack Community edition - making it a better default for student projects. Activity 7 - Floci in the Real Workflow
Showed how Floci fits into a developer loop: write Infrastructure-as-Code or app code locally, spin up emulated AWS resources on the laptop, test interactions in seconds, then push to a shared sandbox only when the design is stable. Highlighted cost and time savings. Activity 8 - Effective Practical Learning Roadmap (3 Phases)
Combined the two tools into a single roadmap. Phase 1 - Mind & Architecture with Cloud Quest; Phase 2 - Code & Fast Testing with Floci; Phase 3 - Real Deployment & Production on real AWS. Students train the right skills with the right tool at the right cost level. Activity 9 - Honest Discussion of Floci Limitations
Listed three real limitations: only a limited set of AWS services is supported, some services return fake or mock results instead of identical behaviour, and it cannot fully replace testing on real AWS for production workloads. Set correct expectations for students. Activity 10 - Cost-Saving Mindset for Student AWS Labs
Wrapping principle: a student should avoid running 24/7 services on real AWS, prefer Cloud Quest for guided learning, use Floci for local iteration, and only deploy to real AWS for final demos or graded milestones. The roadmap makes AWS learning financially safe. Activity 11 - Q&A and Community Discussion
Open floor where students asked about emulator accuracy, pricing for real AWS sandboxes, how to migrate Floci designs to real AWS, and which Cloud Quest roles to start with for a Solutions Architect career path. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Cloud hands-on without fear is possible - choosing the right tool for each learning phase is the real unlock, not avoiding AWS altogether.
Cloud Quest is best for building the architecture mindset: it is guided, gamified and zero-cost - perfect to start before touching Infrastructure-as-Code.
Floci is best for fast iteration: 138x faster than LocalStack and 11x more memory-efficient makes it a serious productivity booster for student projects and CI pipelines.
Cost anxiety is a design problem - using Cloud Quest + Floci + scripted teardown of real AWS labs is the practical answer for students on a tight budget.
Emulators are not magic: limited services, mock results and imperfect fidelity mean real AWS will still be the final test - but you only go there once the design is solid.
Tool layering > tool switching: a 3-phase roadmap (mindset, code, real AWS) is far more effective than picking one emulator or one sandbox and forcing every learning goal into it.
Newly Acquired Skills
Cost-Aware AWS Planning: ability to design hands-on labs in tiers - free tier / emulator / paid sandbox - and pick the right tier for each task. Useful for every future student project and group capstone.
AWS Architecture Mindset: mental model for thinking in services, regions, quotas and dependencies, built through Cloud Quest quests rather than dry document reading.
Local AWS Emulation: hands-on familiarity with Floci - how to spin up emulated AWS resources on a laptop, run interactions locally, and iterate without spending credits.
Tool Benchmarking: ability to compare AWS testing tools on real dimensions (speed, memory, supported services, fidelity) - a skill that also applies to many other engineering tool choices.
Practical Roadmap Design: the meta-skill of breaking a learning goal into phases and matching the cheapest, most effective tool to each phase - directly applicable to self-study and to leading small study groups.
Soft Skills: sharper active listening during English technical talks, structured note-taking on diagrams and benchmark tables, and asking clearer questions about pricing, accuracy and migration between tools.
Closing Note Huynh Thai Linh’s talk was a refreshing reminder that learning AWS as a student is not about who can afford the biggest sandbox account - it is about choosing the right tool for the right phase of learning. By combining AWS Cloud Quest for the architecture mindset and open-source Floci for fast local testing, students can train almost every real-world AWS skill for free, and only graduate to a paid AWS environment when the design is truly production-ready. As a student I walked away with a clearer, cheaper learning roadmap, more confidence in self-directed cloud study, and a concrete playbook I can share with my team and the wider student AWS community.
2. The Iceberg of Procrastination - When Laziness is Just the Tip of Fear A personal, science-backed soft-skill talk delivered inside an AWS Community Day event in Ho Chi Minh City. The session reframed procrastination - not as a time-management problem - but as an emotional regulation problem caused by three hidden layers of fear. The presenter, Khac Uy Pham, then layered on a simple, practical framework (the 5-Minute Rule) and tied everything back to the AWS builder mindset.
Speakers Khac Uy Pham – 3rd Year student at Vietnamese-German University. Summary of content and main activities Khac Uy opened with three painfully honest questions: Has anyone NEVER procrastinated on an important moment? Has anyone scrolled the night before a deadline? Has anyone watched an opportunity - even a personal one - pass by without acting? He then drew the iceberg: only 20-30% of procrastination is the ’lazy’ surface everyone sees (scrolling TikTok, turning off alarms, saying ‘I’ll do it tomorrow’); the hidden 70-80% is fear - of not being good enough, of being judged and of failing. The talk then built a small but powerful toolkit: name the fear, start with five minutes, and let momentum carry the rest, all tied back to the AWS builder philosophy of ‘go build, fail fast, deploy fast, fix early’.
Activity 1 — Opening: A Familiar Enemy
Three interactive questions to break the ice: anyone who never procrastinated, anyone who scrolled before a deadline, anyone who stayed silent when they should have spoken. Established that procrastination is a shared human problem, not a personal failing. Activity 2 — The Iceberg Visual Metaphor
Introduced the iceberg model that anchors the whole talk. The visible tip (laziness) is only 20-30% of the real cause; the hidden mass underwater (fear) is 70-80%. This reframe is the core thesis: ’laziness’ is a surface symptom, not the root cause. Activity 3 — Surface Symptoms of Procrastination
Catalogued the ‘visible’ lazy behaviors: scrolling TikTok / Facebook, turning off alarms and snoozing, saying ‘I’ll do it tomorrow’, low-energy study sessions, last-minute panic. The point: these behaviors are what people see and judge themselves for, but they are not the real driver. Activity 4 — The Hidden Fear: Emotional Regulation, Not Time Management
Cited Prof. Fuschia Sirois (University of Sheffield) and the APA: 70-80% of procrastination is an emotional regulation problem, not a time management problem. Over 80% of adults procrastinate in critical areas like health and career. Biologically, it is the Prefrontal Cortex (logic) vs the Limbic System (emotion), and emotion usually wins. Activity 5 — Layer 1 of Fear: Fear of Not Being Good Enough
Explained the ‘inadequacy’ fear. When a student opens a complex roadmap (Kubernetes, Terraform, a capstone project) and feels overwhelmed, the brain closes the tab and switches to TikTok - instant relief, zero risk of feeling ’not smart enough’. The task never gets started because starting means confronting the feeling. Activity 6 - Layer 2 of Fear: Fear of Judgment
Explained the social-evaluation fear: ‘You’re a Year-3 student and write code like this?’ Many students would rather not submit, not push code, not ask a question in class than risk negative judgment from peers, lecturers or recruiters. Silent procrastination. Activity 7 - Layer 3 of Fear: Fear of Failure
Explained the ‘verdict’ fear: trying and failing feels like a permanent definition of who you are, so not trying is safer. Ironically, not trying is also a verdict - the only difference is that it happens quietly. The feared verdict is delivered either way. Activity 8 - The Guilt Loop
Traced the self-reinforcing cycle: avoid the hard task to feel temporary relief, the relief is followed by guilt, the guilt becomes self-blame, the self-blame becomes stress, and the stress makes the next start even harder - fueling more avoidance. Made the cycle visible so people can step out of it. Activity 9 - The Solution: Action Comes First, Confidence Comes Second
Cited Prof. Timothy Pychyl: emotion follows action, not the other way round. Start before you feel ready; confidence is a by-product of starting, not a prerequisite. This single insight inverts most students’ approach to hard tasks. Activity 10 - Step 1: Name Your Fear
Practical communication refactor. Instead of saying ‘I am lazy’, say ‘I am afraid my code will be judged’, or ‘I am afraid of getting a low grade’, or ‘I am afraid of looking stupid in the demo’. Naming the fear separates ‘I am the problem’ from ‘a specific fear is the problem’ - and a specific fear is something you can act on. Activity 11 - Step 2: The 5-Minute Rule
The most concrete tool from the talk. Pick one tiny starter action (open the laptop, write three lines of code, read one AWS doc page) and commit to only five minutes. Around 90% of the time, once the starter action is done, momentum takes over automatically and you end up working far longer than five minutes. Removes the ‘I have to finish it’ pressure that causes the original avoidance. Activity 12 - The Speaker’s Own ‘YES’ Moment
Khac Uy shared the personal story behind the talk: he was invited to speak at an AWS event, felt completely inadequate, almost said no, paused, recognized that the ‘I am not good enough’ feeling was just the iceberg talking - and said yes anyway. He reframed his own fear, used the 5-Minute Rule to start writing the slides, and shipped the talk you just watched. Activity 13 - How This Connects to the AWS Builder Mindset
Mapped the talk to AWS culture. ‘Go Build’ = start even if it is small. ‘Fail fast’ = lower the cost of being wrong. ‘Deploy fast’ = ship a rough version, don’t wait for perfect. ‘Fix early’ = iterate after deploying, not before. The framework professional builders use is the same framework students can use to defeat procrastination. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Procrastination is an emotional problem wearing a ’time management’ disguise - beating it starts with beating the emotion, not the calendar.
The ’laziness’ story we tell ourselves is almost always wrong - the iceberg is fear, and fear is something you can name, examine and shrink.
Confidence does not come before action - it comes after. Waiting to feel ready is the trap; the only way out is to start small and let the feeling catch up.
A 5-minute commitment is enough to break the guilt loop. The barrier to starting was never the size of the task - it was the size of the commitment in our heads.
Failing has to be reframed as a verb (‘I failed this demo’), not an identity (‘I am a failure’). Identity-based fear is what makes students avoid tasks for years.
Community events like AWS Community Day are a force-multiplier - by simply showing up, you have already broken the start barrier, and you can chain small actions on top of it.
Newly Acquired Skills
Fear-Name Reframing: the ability to translate vague ‘I am lazy’ self-talk into a specific, nameable fear (of judgment, of failure, of not being good enough), which separates identity from behaviour and makes the fear addressable.
5-Minute Rule Habit: a concrete, repeatable micro-commitment framework that can be applied to coding assignments, AWS lab prep, fitness routines, group reports and any task we are avoiding.
Emotional Awareness: better recognition of the guilt-loop signals - instant relief, blame, stress, more avoidance - and the ability to step out of the cycle at the first step instead of the fourth.
Self-Compassion in Tech: learned to treat AWS ‘fail fast’ as an instruction to be kind to my own beginner attempts, rather than as pressure to perform. Lowered the emotional cost of being wrong.
Public-Speaking Courage: internalised the speaker’s own ‘YES’ story and now keep an eye out for similar small opportunities (lightning talks, class demos, community posts) that would normally be skipped.
Soft Skills: sharper listening during a personal, story-driven talk; structured note-taking on a framework that blends psychology, biology and engineering culture; and asking clearer questions about how to apply the framework to my own pending tasks.
Closing Note Khac Uy Pham’s talk hit a nerve that technical talks rarely do. It told students the truth we already half-know: the reason we are not shipping our side projects, not finishing our AWS certifications and not speaking up in class is not laziness - it is an iceberg of fear that we keep calling something else. By giving us a vocabulary (the three fears), a diagnostic (name your fear) and a starter action (the 5-Minute Rule), the session turned an emotional problem into an engineering problem - the kind I can actually solve. As a student I walked away with a calmer story about who I am when I procrastinate, a habit I can apply the same day, and a community reminder that ‘go build’ starts with the next five minutes, nothing more.
3. Why We Always Need Confidence - A Must-Have for Our Careers, Campus Life, and Everything in Between A student-centered soft-skill talk inside the First Cloud AI Journey community track, focused on reframing confidence as a learnable skill rather than an innate trait. The session walked through the cost of self-doubt in student life, the psychology behind imposter syndrome and the Dunning-Kruger effect, the practical benefits of confidence for communication, collaboration and ownership, and a concrete everyday toolkit (preparation, small wins, Mel Robbins’ 5-Second Rule) for building self-trust one moment at a time.
Speakers Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu – student presenter and community learner. Summary of content and main activities Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu opened with a Mark Twain quote about courage being resistance to fear, not absence of fear, then anchored the talk in a deeply relatable student pain point: ‘I knew the tech, but I could not present it’ - strong technical understanding, nervous delivery, underestimated results. She then redefined confidence as ’trying even when nervous’, explicitly not ego and not perfection, framed confidence as a buildable skill, and walked the audience through the science (Imposter Syndrome, Dunning-Kruger, Carol Dweck’s growth mindset) before closing with a small, repeatable daily toolkit: prepare, celebrate small wins and use Mel Robbins’ 5-Second Rule.
Activity 1 — Setting the Frame with Quotes
Opened with Mark Twain: ‘Courage is resistance to fear, mastery of fear - not absence of fear.’ Paired it with Amelia Earhart’s ‘The most difficult thing is the decision to act - the rest is merely tenacity.’ Established that confidence is acting, not feeling ready, before defining any framework. Activity 2 — The Familiar Student Pain: ‘I Knew The Tech, But I Couldn’t Present It’
Shared a candid personal story: solid technical understanding, nervous presentation, underestimated results. Made it explicit that this is a near-universal student experience in labs, demos, capstone reviews, scholarships and interviews, and is the reason the talk exists at all. Activity 3 — Redefining Confidence (Not Ego, Not Perfection)
Three deliberate reframes. Confidence is NOT ego - it is not about being the loudest person in the room. It is NOT perfection - you do not need every answer before you speak. It IS trying even when you feel nervous. This reframe separates posturing from quietly doing the thing anyway. Activity 4 — Technical Skills Are the Foundation, Confidence Is the Bridge
Used a metaphor from the rest of the First Cloud AI Journey: students spend hours on labs, Terraform, Bedrock and dashboards - those technical skills are the foundation, but confidence is the bridge that actually carries those skills out into jobs, internships, group projects and scholarship interviews. Without the bridge, the foundation stays invisible. Activity 5 — he Cost of Self-Doubt in Student Life
Mapped three ‘hidden’ costs students usually do not name. (1) Missed chances: opportunities never even attempted - scholarships, hackathons, talks, internships. (2) Invisible pressure: internal stress that looks calm from the outside but is exhausting. (3) Hidden potential: skills the student has but never shows because they did not raise their hand. Activity 6 — The Science: Imposter Syndrome
Introduced Imposter Syndrome as the persistent feeling of ‘I don’t deserve this’ - even after being admitted to a programme, landing an internship or passing an interview. Showed how it makes students shrink, apologize for their work and avoid new opportunities, magnifying the hidden costs listed in Activity 5. Activity 7 - The Science: Dunning-Kruger Effect
Introduced the Dunning-Kruger Effect as a cognitive bias where beginners feel overconfident and intermediate learners suddenly feel ‘stupid’ as they see how much more there is to learn. Reassured the audience: this dip is a normal stage of real learning, not a sign of being incapable. It is the cost of moving from surface knowledge to deep knowledge. Activity 8 - Carol Dweck’s Growth Mindset
Discussed caching for availability, built-in failover to secondary origins, graceful failure with custom error pages, multi-layer caching (PoP -> Regional Edge Cache -> Origin Shield), HTTP/3 (QUIC/UDP) multiplexing and AWS global backbone for persistent connections. Activity 9 - The Power of Confidence: Connections (Communicate, Collaborate, Connect)
First pillar of confidence’s practical value. Confidence enables you to communicate ideas clearly (presentations, stand-ups, code reviews), collaborate on equal footing with peers and seniors, and connect with mentors, judges and recruiters. Without it, even good work stays isolated. Activity 10 - The Power of Confidence: Ownership
Second pillar. Confidence lets you make decisions in ambiguous situations, defend your ideas in a respectful way (even when challenged), and learn from mistakes without collapsing into self-blame. This is what turns a contributor into an owner in a team. Activity 11 - The Power of Confidence: People Buy the ‘Why’ (Simon Sinek)
Third pillar. Quoted Simon Sinek: ‘People don’t buy what you do, they buy why you do it.’ Why you do something only lands if it is delivered with confident clarity. Confident delivery is therefore a multiplier on every resume line, side project and AWS certification you earn. Activity 12 - Hack #1: Preparation as a Confidence Builder
First practical tool. Preparation creates clarity for presentations, interviews and projects, which reduces fear of the unknown. Concrete examples: rehearse out loud, write your opening line first, walk through the demo before the day of the talk. Activity 13 - Hack #2: Celebrate Small Wins
Second practical tool. Build self-trust by acknowledging small acts: asking a question in class, fixing a bug, sharing an idea in a team meeting, finishing a small AWS lab. Small wins are the raw material confidence is built from, not big milestones. Activity 14 - Hack #3: The 5-Second Rule (Mel Robbins)
Third and most actionable tool. When an opportunity appears (raise your hand, send the application, submit the demo) and self-doubt shows up, count backwards 5-4-3-2-1 and move physically. The countdown interrupts the hesitation loop and turns intention into action. ‘The rest is merely tenacity.’ Activity 15 - Wrap-Up and Final Message
Closing synthesis. Confidence is not something you are born with - it is something you build, one prepared moment and one small win at a time. The talk closed with Brené Brown’s reminder that courage and confidence are verbs - they happen in the small act of moving, not in feeling ready. Final call to action: ‘Act.’ Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Confidence and courage are not the absence of fear - they are the decision to move while fear is still there. Most students wait for the fear to disappear; it almost never does on its own.
Self-doubt has costs I was not naming - missed chances, invisible pressure and hidden potential - and naming them is the first step to stopping them.
The ‘I don’t deserve this’ voice (Imposter Syndrome) is a normal cognitive pattern, not evidence that you actually don’t deserve it. Treating it as data, not verdict, is a real shift.
The mid-learning dip (Dunning-Kruger) means I am actually getting deeper, not getting worse. Re-framing that dip changes how I persist through hard AWS topics like networking, IAM or Terraform.
Carol Dweck’s growth mindset and confidence are the same posture - ‘I can get better’ is inherently more confident than ‘I have to prove I am already great’.
Confidence is a verb you can practise in five minutes (the 5-Second Rule), not a personality trait you hope to inherit.
Newly Acquired Skills
Confidence Reframing: the ability to translate ‘I am not confident’ into a specific barrier (nervous delivery, unprepared slot, fear of judgment) and pick the matching hack. Confidence stops being a abstract character trait and becomes a concrete fix.
Imposter Syndrome Awareness: recognising the ‘I don’t deserve this’ voice in myself and in teammates as a common pattern, and learning to talk back to it with statements like ’lots of qualified people feel this, it is not evidence’. Useful in interviews, scholarship reviews and group presentations.
Dunning-Kruger Navigation: practical understanding of the mid-learning dip and the discipline to keep pushing through it instead of concluding ‘I am bad at this’. Especially useful for AWS, DevOps and certification study where intermediate material feels overwhelming.
Growth Mindset Communication: learned to talk about skills in ‘I can get better’ language rather than ‘I am a person who is/can’t’ language, both in self-talk and when giving feedback to teammates.
5-Second Rule Practise: a concrete, body-based intervention (count 5-4-3-2-1, then move) that interrupts hesitation in real time - already used to send in a class application and to raise a hand in Q&A.
Soft Skills: better active listening during a personal, story-driven talk; structured note-taking on psychology frameworks; asking clearer questions about how to support peers who show low confidence in group settings.
Closing Note Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu’s talk was a quiet but firm reminder that confidence is not something I either have or do not have - it is something I have been quietly building or quietly eroding with every small act of showing up or staying silent. By naming Imposter Syndrome and the Dunning-Kruger dip, by quoting Carol Dweck, Simon Sinek and Brené Brown, and by handing us the 5-Second Rule as a tiny but real tool, the session turned an emotional topic into one I can practise - in the next class, the next stand-up and the next scholarship application. As a student I walked away with a kinder story about who I am when I am nervous, a habit I can apply the same day, and a clearer responsibility to support classmates who are visibly carrying the same invisible weight.
4. The Hidden Iceberg of a Project - DevOps Before Disaster A technical-and-culture talk that uses the iceberg metaphor to explain why software projects fail in slow, painful ways - and how DevOps, understood as a mindset (People, Process, Technology) and not just a toolset, can address the hidden causes below the surface before they turn into a project-level disaster. The session blends systems thinking, DevOps principles and very concrete tooling (Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, Cloud, Terraform) with candid advice on team culture and ownership.
Speakers Tran Minh Quan – community speaker and DevOps practitioner. Summary of content and main activities Tran Minh Quan opened with a painfully familiar story: a feature that was estimated at two days triggered three reworks, endless misunderstandings and team-wide exhaustion. He used that story to introduce the iceberg model of a project - in which only symptoms (missed deadlines, bugs, failed deployments, complaints, burnout) are visible above the water, while the true causes (ambiguous requirements, communication gaps, siloed teams, lack of ownership, manual processes, slow feedback loops) stay underwater and slowly sink the project. He then contrasted the symptom-fixing mindset with system thinking, framed DevOps as People + Process + Technology (not just tooling), laid out four pillars (collaboration, automation, fast feedback, continuous improvement), mapped each hidden cause to its DevOps solution and closed with the Titanic warning: small ignored warnings become big disasters if you only fix what’s visible.
Activity 1 — Opening Story: The ‘Simple Feature’ That Ate the Sprint
Set the tone with a real scenario: two-day estimate, three reworks, endless misunderstandings and a project that feels exhausted. Made the audience recognise their own team in the story before the iceberg model was introduced. Activity 2 — Framing the Iceberg Model of a Project
Introduced the central analogy. Above water: the symptoms the manager and the customer see. Below water: the slow, structural causes that quietly accumulate. Without diving below the surface, no amount of firefighting can save the project. Activity 3 — The Tip of the Iceberg - Visible Symptoms
Catalogued what teams usually blame: missed deadlines, production bugs, failed deployments, customer complaints, team burnout. These are real - and painful - but they are the output of the system, not the cause. Activity 4 - Beneath the Surface - Hidden Causes
Mapped six structural causes: requirement ambiguity (vague tickets), communication gaps (handoff losses between dev, QA and ops), siloed teams (dev vs. ops vs. QA), lack of ownership (no one owns the whole flow), manual processes (deploys by hand), slow feedback loops (bugs caught weeks after commit). Activity 5 — Why Solving Symptoms Is Not Enough
Contrasted two mindsets. Firefighting / Symptom Fixing = ‘fix the deadline, fix the bug, fix the deploy’. System Thinking = ‘why did the deadline slip? why did the bug reach production? why did the deploy break?’. Without the second, the same symptoms return in a different shape every sprint. Activity 6 — DevOps as the Solution: People, Process, Technology
Defined DevOps not as a tool list but as a triangle. People: culture, ownership, shared responsibility. Process: how work moves from idea to production. Technology: the tools that support the people and process. Quoted the speaker’s reminder that you can automate deployments but you cannot automate trust, communication or ownership. Activity 7 - Pillar 1: Collaboration (Breaking Silos)
First pillar. Move from blame culture (dev blames QA, QA blames ops, ops blames dev) to shared responsibility and shared goals. Practical signals: cross-functional teams, joint incident reviews (not post-mortems designed to assign blame), shared Slack channels for product + infra. Activity 8 - Pillar 2: Automation (Reducing Friction and Human Error)
Second pillar. Reduce manual hand-offs through CI/CD, Infrastructure as Code, automated tests and automated workflows. The point is not ‘more scripts’ but ‘fewer places a human can forget a step’ - reworks disappear when the pipeline enforces quality. Activity 9 - Pillar 3: Fast Feedback (See Problems Earlier)
Third pillar. Push discovery as early as possible through monitoring, observability, continuous integration, feature flags and canary releases. The cost of finding a bug grows exponentially with time - fast feedback collapses that cost back down. Activity 10 - Pillar 4: Continuous Improvement (Learning, Not Repeating)
Fourth pillar. Treat failures as data. Use retrospectives, blameless post-mortems and concrete metrics to learn from incidents and near-misses. The opposite culture - hiding mistakes and re-running the same playbook - is what sinks projects slowly. Activity 11 - Mapping Hidden Causes to DevOps Solutions
Drew an explicit cause-to-pillar map: requirement ambiguity is reduced by collaboration (shared refinement); communication gaps by collaboration (joint channels, demos); siloed teams by shared ownership; manual processes by automation; slow feedback loops by fast feedback; repeated failures by continuous improvement. Made the framework operational, not just philosophical. Activity 12 - Tooling Reality Check (Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, Cloud, Terraform)
Acknowledged the tools students and teams actually use: Docker for packaging, Kubernetes for orchestration, CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions / Jenkins / GitLab CI) for automation, cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) for infrastructure, Terraform for IaC. Re-framed them as enablers of the four pillars, not as the definition of DevOps. Activity 13 - Adopting the Mindset in a Student Team (Low Budget)
Practical advice for the audience. You do not need a Kubernetes cluster to start: start with a shared README that codifies ‘how we deploy’, a simple CI pipeline on a free tier, demo on Friday so feedback is fast, and a weekly 15-minute retro where the goal is learning and not blame. The DevOps mindset is largely free. Activity 14 - The Titanic Connection - Small Warnings, Big Disasters
Closing metaphor. The Titanic did not sink because of a single huge failure - it sank because many small warnings (lookouts, radio messages, lifeboat capacity, speed in ice fields) were each individually manageable and collectively ignored. Software projects fail for the same reason: ignored warnings accumulate under the surface until a single feature slip or production bug becomes a disaster. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Symptoms and causes are not the same thing - missing a deadline is not the problem, it is the visible output of much deeper structural issues. Reframing ‘who is late’ into ‘what system made this late’ is the most useful shift in any failing student project.
DevOps is a cultural and process movement first, a tooling movement second - buying Kubernetes does not fix a broken handover between dev and ops, but shared ownership does.
The four pillars (collaboration, automation, fast feedback, continuous improvement) are not independent - they reinforce each other. Automation without collaboration just speeds up a wrong process.
Small warnings ignored for too long become big disasters - the Titanic analogy is not just a story, it is a project-management heuristic. Track near-misses, not only incidents.
Student teams on no budget can still adopt the DevOps mindset: a shared README, a simple CI pipeline, a small weekly retro and a Friday demo cover a surprising amount of the iceberg.
Tooling has its place but is not the destination - Docker + Terraform + GitHub Actions are enablers for the four pillars, not definitions of DevOps.
Newly Acquired Skills
Iceberg Diagnosis Skill: the ability to take an obvious project symptom (missed deadline, broken deploy, recurring bug) and ask ‘which hidden cause below the surface produced this?’ until the real driver is identified, before deciding on a fix.
DevOps Mental Model: a clean understanding of People / Process / Technology, plus the four pillars, that can be applied to any team setting - not only enterprise DevOps shops. Useful for capstone projects, hackathons, club roles and internship teams.
Cause-to-Pillar Mapping: practical mapping skill: pick a hidden cause, find the matching pillar, design a small intervention. E.g. manual deploys map to automation, team-silo issues map to collaboration.
Blameless Retro Habit: introduced a 15-minute weekly retro focused on learning rather than blame - a concrete implementation of the ‘continuous improvement’ pillar that works for student teams.
Lightweight CI/CD Awareness: familiarity with how to set up a basic CI pipeline (GitHub Actions / GitLab CI) on the free tier, with IaC concepts (Terraform) that can be studied incrementally, and with Docker as the simplest way to make ‘works on my laptop’ problems disappear.
Soft Skills: sharper listening during a hybrid technical/cultural talk, structured note-taking on a framework that spans process and code, and clearer questions about how to introduce cultural change in a peer-led student team.
Closing Note Tran Minh Quan’s talk was a quiet but firm reminder that most student projects do not fail because of a single bad line of code or one careless teammate - they fail because the iceberg below the surface (ambiguous requirements, broken handoffs, silos, manual processes, slow feedback) is allowed to accumulate while the team only fights the visible symptoms. By handing us a diagnosis tool (the iceberg model) and a treatment framework (the four DevOps pillars mapped to hidden causes), the session turned an abstract cultural topic into something I can probe in our next retro. As a student I walked away with a calmer, more structural way of looking at project failure, a few concrete habits (Friday demos, blameless retro, a shared README) I can apply the same week, and a much clearer answer to the question ‘what is DevOps really?’.
5. The Ballers - FCAJ Challenger Hackathon Experience Sharing A student-team lightning talk hosted inside the First Cloud AI Journey community track, shared by the three-member team ‘The Ballers’ right after their participation in the FCAJ Challenger Hackathon. The session explained what a hackathon really is (beyond the brochure), why every student should join at least one, what the team actually went through during the 24-48 hour sprint (the fun parts and the painful ones), the technical and physical lessons they extracted from it, and a direct invitation to future students to join them in the next round.
Speakers ** The Ballers team - Huynh An Khuong, Mai Quoc Anh and Nguyen Tran Minh Quan.** Summary of content and main activities The Ballers opened with the four-part promise of the talk (what is a hackathon, why you should join, our experiences, join with us) and a tongue-in-cheek subtitle that set the tone for the rest of the session: ’tons of fun, of bug fixes, of back pains, of sleep deprivation.’ They then defined a hackathon honestly - a 24-48 hour, fast-paced event where you tackle a real-world problem, ship an MVP, and rely on collaboration, creativity and quick iteration. They laid out the benefits (learn by doing, portfolio, networking) and the actual lived experience (no place to sleep, no 24/7 air-conditioned cafe, fast food, energy drinks, dirty floors, taken-over beanbags, last-minute document rewrites due to a website error and very long processing times). They closed with five hard-earned lessons and an irreverent, very human call to action that included their own unofficial motto: ‘Are you flexing your credits? I’m stealing your Agora credits.’
Activity 1 — Team Self-Introduction: The Ballers
Speaker line-up: Huynh An Khuong, Mai Quoc Anh and Nguyen Tran Minh Quan. Three students, one shared brain-cell, one shared Google Doc, one shared deadline. Started the talk as a team, not as three separate individuals - immediately modelling the collaboration spirit they were about to advocate. Activity 2 - The Promise of the Talk (Four-Part Roadmap)
The team laid out the agenda up front: (1) what is a hackathon; (2) why you should join one; (3) our actual experiences (good and bad); (4) join with us in the next one. Set audience expectations so the messy stories in section three would land properly. Activity 3 — Defining a Hackathon Honestly
Demystified the term. A hackathon is a fast-paced event where you build a solution in a limited time frame (typically 24-48 hours). You tackle a real-world problem, focus on creating a prototype or MVP, and rely on collaboration, creativity and quick iteration. No corporate-speak, no ‘idea competition’ confusion. Activity 4 - Reframing the Hackathon Flow
Showed the ‘common mental model’ they want to break: Show up → build stuff → go home. The reality is longer and messier - Show up → fight for a beanbag → debug for hours → eat questionable fast food → ship something → go home. Same length, different density. Activity 5 — Why You (Specifically) Should Join - Learning and Portfolio
First benefits block. Learn by doing: a hackathon is the fastest way to turn theory into practice. Not a real project yet, but it is a real prototype, and a real prototype is something you can put on your CV and your GitHub. For students with little work experience, this is the highest-leverage line you can add to a resume. Activity 6 — Why You (Specifically) Should Join - Networking and Teamwork
Second benefits block. A hackathon is the rare event where developers, designers and business-minded students are in the same room for 48 hours. Recruiters and mentors are also present and genuinely approachable. Some of the loudest ’networking wins’ in student careers come from a 2am pizza break, not a career fair. Activity 7 - The Real Fun: Chaos, Pizza and Weird Ideas
Made it explicit that ‘fun’ is part of the value. The chaotic brainstorming, the absurd feature ideas at 3am and the shared pizza boxes are not distractions - they are how the team actually bonds, and they are part of why most hackathon alumni go back. Activity 8 - The Physical Reality: Where to Sleep
First ‘painful’ story. No dedicated place to sleep; zero 24/7 open cafes in the area with AC. The team tried to nap on beanbags only to discover they had all been taken by an army of 8-12 year old Minecraft players. Brutal, hilarious, and a real planning point for future attendees. Activity 9 - The Physical Reality: Food, Energy and Backs
Continued the honest recount. No sleep, only fast food, only energy drinks. Back pains from sitting on the floor. Dirty floors, no proper chair setup. The team urged the audience to bring a small pillow, a water bottle and (if possible) a real chair cushion. Activity 10 - The Technical Reality: Last-Minute Website Failure
First ’technical’ war story. Their website broke near the end of the sprint due to a platform error, forcing the team to rewrite the supporting documentation in the final hours - a real lesson in always keeping the documentation decoupled from the deployed app. Activity 11 - The Technical Reality: Long Processing and Sponsor Bugs
Continued the technical recount. Very long processing times for some core operations, plus a sponsor-provided audio clip that ‘sounds too real even for a human’ (a humourous way of saying it was clearly machine-generated). Reinforced that hackathons will expose every weakness in your stack and that you have to build buffer time for that. Activity 12 - Lesson 1: Start with a Real Problem (Pain Point)
Lesson one of five. The projects that win - and the projects the team remembers - are the ones anchored in a real pain point, not in a clever tech demo. Pick a problem you or your team actually feels, then pick the smallest technology that could solve it. Activity 13 - Lesson 2: Persistence Matters
Lesson two. Most of what fails during a hackathon is not the technology - it is your stamina. Be ready to keep going when the build breaks at 2am. Persistence is the most transferable hackathon skill, not the framework you used. Activity 14 - Lesson 3: Experiment Relentlessly
Lesson three. The 48 hours is too short to over-think. Try things quickly, throw away what does not work, keep what does. The team joked that they ‘killed’ at least three approaches before settling on the final one - that iteration is the point. Activity 15 - Lesson 4: Be Resourceful (Combine Ideas)
Lesson four. Hackathons reward resourceful combinations, not original inventions. Steal good patterns from open-source, remix sponsor APIs, combine tools you already know. The Agora credits the Ballers joked about stealing is, in their words, exactly this: borrow every free resource the sponsors give you. Activity 16 - Lesson 5: Fully Utilize Tools
Lesson five. Hackathon sponsors give you credits, SDKs and APIs for a reason - because they want you to actually use them. The Ballers’ off-hand ‘Are you flexing your credits, son? I’m stealing your Agora credits’ was their way of saying: if you joined, use what is offered. Activity 17 - Final Call to Action: Join with Us
Closing pitch. The Ballers invited the audience to team up for the next FCAJ Hackathon, promised an equally chaotic experience and pointed out that first-timers are always welcome. Tied the talk back to its subtitle and ended with the now-famous ‘Join with us’ slide. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
A hackathon is not a coding contest for elite developers - it is a 24-48 hour lab where students can convert theory into a working prototype in a way no tutorial or course assignment allows.
The brochure never mentions the messy parts - no sleep, fast food, back pains, last-minute document rewrites, sponsor bugs. Going in with that picture already in mind removes a huge amount of first-timer anxiety.
Starting with a real pain point beats starting with a clever tech demo. Judges and mentors remember the projects that solve a problem they have, not the ones that flex a framework.
Persistence is the most transferable skill you build at a hackathon - the willingness to keep going when the build breaks at 2am is what separates finished prototypes from abandoned ones.
Resourcefulness beats originality. Use sponsor credits, remix open-source patterns, combine APIs - hackathons reward pragmatic mashups, not inventions from scratch.
Networking at a hackathon is real and immediate - recruiters, mentors and senior developers are physically present and approachable. A 2am pizza conversation can open more doors than a polished cover letter.
Newly Acquired Skills
Hackathon Literacy: a clear mental model of what a hackathon actually is (24-48 hours, MVP, real problem, collaboration + iteration) vs. what the marketing brochures imply. Removes the ‘is this for me?’ hesitation for first-timers.
Problem-First Ideation: the ability to pick a project idea by first naming the user’s pain point, then picking the smallest technology that could solve it - reusable in capstone, scholarship and internship pitches.
Hackathon Survival Planning: a practical prep checklist (sleep mask, water bottle, snacks, portable cushion, decoupled documentation) drawn directly from the team’s painful experience, applicable to any multi-day sprint.
Resource-Combining Mindset: the habit of asking ‘what free tool or sponsor credit already exists for this?’ before building from scratch. Useful for any constrained budget student project.
Resilience Under Pressure: internalised the speakers’ 2am-rebuild stories, raising my own tolerance for shipping in imperfect conditions and accepting ‘good enough’ as a valid finishing state.
Soft Skills: sharper listening during a high-energy, story-driven talk; structured note-taking that separates ‘fun stories’ from ’transferable lessons’; asking clearer questions about how to form a team and pick an idea as a true beginner.
Closing Note The Ballers’ talk was a refreshingly honest, almost disarmingly funny recount of what a real student hackathon actually feels like - not the brochure version. By giving us both the upside (learning, portfolio, network, pizza-fuelled chaos) and the unglamorous downside (no sleep, fast food, last-minute rewrites, sponsor bugs), the session lowered the imaginary barrier between ‘people who do hackathons’ and ‘people who don’t’. As a first-time observer I walked away with a clear, candid picture of what to expect, a small set of habits to bring into any multi-day build, and - most importantly - a concrete decision to actually sign up for the next round with friends instead of just watching from the sidelines.

A Comprehensive Astrology Platform for the New Generation - Combining Traditional Knowledge with Modern Technology A workshop-style talk inside the First Cloud AI Journey ‘Spiritual Tech’ community track, delivered by Tran Huu Nghia. The presentation made the case that global interest in astrology, tarot and metaphysical self-care is no longer a niche hobby but a fast-growing technology segment, and that a serious ’next-generation’ platform must combine classical knowledge (astronomical calculations, Vedic and Western traditions, five elements, lunar calendar) with modern technology (AI-assisted interpretation, real-time natal charts, NLP-driven personalisation, mobile-first UX). The session framed the audience problem, analysed global competitors (Co-Star, Sanctuary, The Pattern, Astrology.com, Nebula), unpacked the technical architecture (real-time natal chart engine, fine-tuned LLM, compatibility engine, daily horoscope pipeline, social/community features), discussed monetisation (freemium, premium subscription, consulting marketplace, B2B API) and closed with a localisation strategy for the Vietnamese market.
Speakers Tran Huu Nghia Summary of content and main activities Tran Huu Nghia opened by reframing astrology from ‘fortune-telling’ to ‘objective psychological self-analysis’ for a generation that is anxious, digitally saturated and increasingly detached from traditional religious institutions. He cited global research on Gen-Z and Millennial mental health, anchored the talk in concrete market-size numbers (4.7 to 11+ billion USD market size, 14 to 20 percent CAGR), then moved into a technical deep dive: a real-time natal chart calculation engine connected to the NASA JPL planetary database, a fine-tuned LLM trained on classical Sanskrit and Western astrological texts plus real-world consultation conversations, a compatibility engine that goes far beyond sun-sign comparison and a daily-horoscope pipeline personalised to each user’s birth data. He then teardown-ana lysed four global competitors, mapped monetisation paths (freemium, consulting, B2B API), and closed with a localisation plan tailored to Vietnamese cultural context.
Activity 1 — Opening: The Growth Era of the Spiritual Tech Segment
Set the framing. Astrology and Western occult disciplines are emerging as a serious knowledge system for the new generation - not a fortune-telling booth, but an alternative lens for personal identity in an anxious era. Reframed the audience as young builders rather than believers. Activity 2 — The Gen Zennial Mental-Health Backdrop
Anchored the demand in hard numbers. Up to 57 percent of Gen-Z and 46 percent of Millennials in the United States regularly experience anxiety or depression symptoms. Mobile saturation and constant connection create psychological pressure, and traditional religious institutions are losing appeal with young users - which opens the door for astrology as a non-judgmental self-analysis tool. Activity 3 — Global Market Size and CAGR (Multi-Source Forecast)
Presented four reputable market reports in one slide to make the commercial potential tangible. Research and Markets: 4.73B in 2025 to 11.71B in 2030 at 20.2 percent CAGR. Report Cubes: 4.75B in 2025 to 25.44B in 2034 at 20.5 percent CAGR. MarkNtel Advisors: 3.00B in 2024 to 9.00B in 2030 at 20 percent CAGR. Market Mind Partners: 3.20B in 2025 to 9.80B in 2033 at 14.8 percent CAGR. Subscription and consulting contribute up to 45 percent of revenue. Activity 4 - Technical Pillar 1: Real-Time Natal Chart Calculation Engine
Walked through the astrophysics side. Accurate natal charts need the precise geometric positions of celestial bodies at the user’s exact birth time and geographical coordinates. The proposed ecosystem pulls planetary coordinates from NASA’s National Jet Propulsion Laboratory database and converts them to ecliptic coordinates using either the Tropical (Western) or Sidereal (Vedic) zodiac system, depending on the user’s preference. Activity 5 — Technical Pillar 2: Fine-Tuned LLM on Classical and Conversational Data
Showed where the AI layer sits on top of the chart engine. The platform fine-tunes a foundational model (such as GPT) using three datasets. (1) Classical texts - thousands of pages of traditional astrological knowledge, including Sanskrit translations of Vedic material. (2) Real-world consultation data - anonymised conversations between users and professional astrologers, used to teach the model to be empathetic and psychologically aware. (3) User behaviour data - mood logs, feedback and historical interactions used to continuously refine responses. Activity 6 - Technical Pillar 3: Multidimensional Compatibility Engine
Moved beyond sun-sign matching. The engine analyses emotional compatibility, communication-style fit, lifestyle rhythm, conflict handling and long-term alignment using both the chart engine and the fine-tuned LLM. Output is a structured narrative that is far richer than the ‘compatible / not compatible’ verdict traditional apps return. Activity 7 - Technical Pillar 4: Daily Personalised Horoscope Pipeline
Explained the daily loop. Each morning the system recomputes the user’s transit chart (current sky vs. natal positions) and writes a personalised reading for the day, tuned to what the user actually cares about (career, relationships, study, health, mood). The brief is then expanded by the LLM into a 60-second read that feels like it was written for the user - not like a generic horoscope column. Activity 8 - Competitive Landscape: Co-Star
First competitor analysed. Co-Star dominates Western markets with hyper-modern minimalist design, AI-driven readings and strong social features (friends’ chart comparison). Strengths: brand recognition and UX taste. Weaknesses: opaque data model, Western-only astrology, no Vietnamese localisation, no consulting layer. Activity 9 - Competitive Landscape: Sanctuary
Second competitor. Sanctuary leads with live human astrologer chat - real-time consultation rather than algorithmic output. Strengths: human warmth and trust. Weaknesses: high variable cost (real astrologers), limited personalisation depth, limited offline content and language coverage. Activity 10 - Competitive Landscape: The Pattern and Astrology.com
Third pair. The Pattern focuses on personality archetypes and compatibility (psychology-meets-astrology), with long retention through daily prompts. Astrology.com is the legacy aggregator with the largest content library but dated UX. Both show that ‘personality over prediction’ is what hooks users long-term. Activity 11 - Monetisation Strategy (Freemium, Premium, Marketplace, B2B API)
Laid out four revenue streams. (1) Freemium entry - free daily horoscope and a basic natal chart. (2) Premium subscription - deep compatibility reports, year-ahead forecasts, AI companion mode. (3) Consulting marketplace - verified human astrologers with revenue share. (4) B2B API - white-label chart engine and reading engine for media publishers, dating apps and HR-tech platforms. Activity 12 - Localisation Strategy for Vietnam (Language and Cultural)
Adaptation layer. Translate every micro-copy into natural, idiomatic Vietnamese rather than literal translation. Blend Vietnamese folk astrology references (can chi, ngay tot xau, zodiac animals) with Western and Vedic frameworks so the app feels rooted. Avoid astrology-religious-confusion by clearly differentiating the app from fortune-telling - position it as ‘psychological self-analysis’ instead. Activity 13 - Cultural and Ethical Guardrails
Discussed the risks. Designed guardrails against medical, legal and financial advice. Added disclaimers for vulnerable users. Added a ‘pause’ UX that surfaces professional mental-health resources when a user’s text indicates possible distress. Made cultural sensitivity a first-class product requirement, not a post-launch patch. Activity 14 - MVP Roadmap (90 Days)
Concretised the next quarter. Days 0-30: ship the chart engine (Tropical only) and a basic Vietnamese daily horoscope. Days 30-60: add compatibility engine v1 and onboarding tune (birth-time capture, location auto-complete). Days 60-90: ship the AI companion mode with the fine-tuned LLM in shadow mode for evaluation. Activity 15 - Go-To-Market and Community-First Distribution
Community-first growth. Launch in student creator communities on TikTok and YouTube Vietnam, partner with astrology YouTubers for honest product reviews, build an in-app ‘share to friends’ loop that turns compatibility reports into organic growth. Avoid paid-acquisition-heavy launch until retention is proven. Activity 16 - Q&A and Feedback from Founders
Open floor. Founders and students asked about data-privacy on birth-time collection, content moderation on the consulting marketplace, and how to keep the AI-generated readings from feeling generically positive. The standing advice was: ship the chart engine first, get honest user feedback, then scale the AI. Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
Astrology is a real technology segment today, not a hobby - with 14 to 20 percent CAGR across multiple reputable forecasts and double-digit-billion-dollar end values. Students who treat it as a startup domain are looking at a real market, not a quirk.
An ‘AI-native astrology app’ is essentially three engines glued together - a real-time natal chart engine (NASA / JPL data), a fine-tuned LLM on classical + conversational data, and a compatibility engine. Modelling the system this way clarifies what to build first and what to buy, defer or partner for.
Personalisation beats prediction. Apps that focus on personality archetypes and compatibility retain users far longer than apps that focus on daily horoscope predictions alone.
Cultural localisation is a feature, not a wrapper. Literal translation and Western default zodiac systems kill adoption in Vietnam - blending traditional knowledge (can chi, zodiac animals, ngay tot xau) with Western and Vedic frameworks is what makes the product feel rooted.
Ethical and cultural guardrails have to be designed in on day one. Medical, legal and financial advice should be blocked. Vulnerable users deserve a graceful handoff to real support resources. Treating ethics as a post-launch patch is a recipe for incident-driven damage.
Community-first distribution beats paid acquisition at this stage. The category is social by nature - compatibility reports are designed to be shared, and a thoughtful creator-partner loop is more cost-efficient than performance marketing for an MVP.
Newly Acquired Skills
Spiritual-Tech Market Reading: the ability to read and reconcile multi-source market-size forecasts, understand subscription-heavy revenue mix and recognise when a category is mainstream enough for product investment. Directly applicable to future product thinking regardless of category.
Astrology Tech Stack Mental Model: a working picture of how a modern astrology platform is engineered - chart engine with NASA / JPL coordinates, ecliptic conversion between Tropical and Sidereal systems, fine-tuned LLM, compatibility engine, daily transit pipeline. Strong blueprint for any AI-on-classical-data product.
Cross-Tradition Astrology Literacy: real awareness of how Western, Vedic and Vietnamese folk astrology systems differ (Tropical vs. Sidereal zodiac base, can chi cycles, twelve-animal zodiac). Lets me reason about cultural localisation rather than blindly translate UX strings.
Competitive Teardown Habit: a structured way to compare astrology competitors on dimensions like UX, personalisation depth, content model and human-vs-AI balance. Transferable to teardown any consumer product category.
Cultural-Sensitivity Product Design: concrete methods for designing ethical guardrails (medical/legal/financial advice, vulnerable-user handoff, anti-fortune-telling positioning) into a consumer spiritual app from day one.
Soft Skills: sharper listening during a hybrid business + technical + cultural talk; structured note-taking that separated ‘market size’, ’tech stack’ and ’localisation’; clearer questions about fine-tuning cost, content moderation and launch strategy.
Closing Note Tran Huu Nghia talk did something that is rare in a student meetup: it took a category Vietnamese students often think of as soft content and showed that it can be engineered with the same rigour we put into any other AI product - a real-time chart engine, a fine-tuned LLM, a careful compatibility pipeline, an ethical wrapper and a community-first growth plan. The dual message - that traditional knowledge deserves modern treatment, and that modern technology has to respect the culture it serves - is one I will carry into my next product decision. As a student I walked away with a mental model for Spiritual Tech products, a sharper sense of how Vietnamese cultural context should shape product decisions and a quiet reminder that the next big category-defining app is very likely to come from someone treating a ‘soft’ category with hard engineering.
7. How Cloud Architecture Powers Modern Multiplayer Games A technical deep-dive seminar inside the First Cloud AI Journey community track, delivered by Pham Quang Thai. The talk framed the explosive growth of online multiplayer gaming and the engineering problem it creates: simultaneously demanding real-time interaction with minimal latency AND the ability to instantly scale to millions of concurrent players. The seminar dissected infrastructure models from Amazon Web Services (GameLift, FlexMatch, FleetIQ), Google Cloud Platform (GKE, Agones with Ubisoft, Quilkin), Microsoft Azure (complex demand forecasting) and Oracle Cloud, and closed with future trends in edge computing, cloud gaming with GPU servers and Generative AI for virtual worlds.
Speakers Pham Quang Thai Summary of content and main activities Pham Quang Thai opened with a clear engineering picture of the modern multiplayer industry and the dual pressure it puts on infrastructure: real-time interaction with minimal latency AND elastic scaling for millions of concurrent sessions. He categorised the problem into two architectural shapes - session-based (FPS, MOBA, Battle Royale, low-latency dedicated game servers) and persistent-world (MMOs, stateful, AI-heavy) - and walked through the AWS (GameLift, FlexMatch, FleetIQ), Google Cloud (GKE, Agones, Quilkin), Microsoft Azure and Oracle Cloud stacks that power them today. He spent the middle of the talk on the matchmaking flow (edge latency via ping endpoints stored in DynamoDB, API Gateway plus JWT authentication, AWS Lambda decentralised processing, RTT minimisation), the elasticity pattern via Agones ‘Allocated’ states and Spot / Preemptible instances managed by a FleetIQ-style predictive algorithm, and the security stack (REPs at the edge, DynamoDB rate-limiting writes at up to 800,000 writes per 30 seconds, Kinesis to S3 plus Athena SQL queries for cheater detection). He closed with real-game references (Valorant and the Tran Huu Nghia ‘SouhcNi’ mention on millisecond latency jitter) and three forward-looking vectors: edge computing (CloudFront), cloud gaming with GPU-equipped servers under 100 ms interaction latency over RTP/SRTP, and Generative AI for virtual-world creation powered by Amazon SageMaker.
Activity 1 — Setting the Industry Stage: The Multiplayer Pressure Problem
Opened with the modern multiplayer industry’s growth wave and the dual engineering constraint it creates: real-time interaction with minimal latency AND instant scaling to handle millions of concurrent players. The audience appreciated that a single bad architecture choice is now visible to every player, every minute. Activity 2 — Categorising Game Architecture - Session-Based
First architectural shape. Session-Based Architecture focuses on rapidly creating Dedicated Game Servers (DGS), matchmaking based on user latency, transient state and battle-style flow. Typical genres: FPS, MOBA, Battle Royale. Strict latency target: 50 ms or less per round trip. Activity 3 — Categorising Game Architecture - Persistent-World (MMO)
Second architectural shape. Sustainable World Architecture demands enormous CPU for AI-driven simulation of thousands of entities in a continuous world. MMORPG servers typically run physics and character-state simulation at a 30 Hz Tick Rate. Requires stateful databases for permanent storage of player assets, levels and inventory. Activity 4 - AWS Stack Walkthrough - GameLift and FlexMatch
Dove into the AWS offering. Amazon GameLift manages the full server lifecycle - spin-up, replace, retire. FlexMatch runs custom matchmaking rules (latency, skill, team composition). The combination is the de-facto backbone of many Western session-based games. Activity 5 — Matchmaking Flow Step-by-Step (Latency and Edge Detection)
Walked through the matchmaking pipeline. Step 1: edge latency measurement - the client probes endpoints stored in DynamoDB. Step 2: SDK request initiation via API Gateway with JWT authentication as a JSON Web Token. Step 3: AWS Lambda runs decentralised matchmaking logic. Step 4: optimal region chosen via a mathematical RTT (Round-Trip Time) minimisation formula selecting the cloud region with the lowest average latency across the group. Activity 6 - Elasticity Pattern: Agones ‘Allocated’ State on Kubernetes
Explained the killer Agones pattern. When a session is matched, Agones flips the GameServer into an ‘Allocated’ state, which prevents Kubernetes from scaling it down during an active match. Quilkin, an open-source UDP proxy, supports low-latency real-time traffic. Together they solve the race condition between scale-down and active match. Activity 7 - Cost Optimisation: FleetIQ, Spot and Preemptible Instances
Showed the cost lever. FleetIQ continuously runs predictive algorithms to assess the stability of Spot partitions, then places game servers on the cheapest reliable Spot capacity. Reported saving: 70 to 80 percent vs on-demand. Critical insight: saving money on game infrastructure is a continuous stability-vs-cost tradeoff, not a one-time deal. Activity 8 - Google Cloud, Azure and Oracle Cloud Differentiators
Brief on the other major clouds. Google Kubernetes Engine plus Agones allows multi-cloud, fully open-source, vendor-locked-free deployments. Azure focuses on complex demand forecasting models to optimise inventory and resource allocation, borrowed from large-scale supply chain systems. Oracle Cloud is a competent alternative for cost-sensitive deployments with strong database roots. Cross-cloud flexibility is a real strategic lever for studios. Activity 9 - Engineering Latency Budget (50 ms for Session-Based)
Distilled the latency target. For session-based games, the practical latency budget is 50 ms or less per round trip. Anything beyond that consistently produces a poor player experience, even with great graphics. The closest reference to this is the SouhcNi (Tran Huu Nghia) mention from a Valorant case study about millisecond-level latency jitter. Activity 10 - Security Layer 1: Remote Entry Points (REPs) as DDoS Shields
First security layer. Remote Entry Points (REPs) are deployed as boundary shields at the network edge. They hide the private VPC grid from the public internet, absorb DDoS attacks, and ensure that player traffic only reaches the real game servers after the REP has validated the session. Activity 11 - Security Layer 2: DynamoDB Writes and Anti-Fraud
Second security layer. A distributed NoSQL database like Amazon DynamoDB records every state change - up to 800,000 writes per 30 seconds in a typical live game. Rate limiting at this layer prevents item duplication exploits (the ‘buy once, receive twice’ hack) by ensuring each transaction is uniquely recorded. Activity 12 - Security Layer 3: Kinesis, S3 and Athena Cheat Detection
Third security layer. Standard data processing pipelines use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to push telemetry into S3, then Amazon Athena runs SQL queries directly on that lake to analyse anomalous behaviour, detect hacks, cheats and transaction anomalies. Real-time observability plus retrospective forensics from the same data. Activity 13 - Asset and State Synchronisation (Live Service Backbone)
Addressed the persistent problem. All changes to player assets, levels and equipment must be continuously synchronised between the client and the server across thousands of concurrent players. This is the operational definition of ’live service’ gaming and the source of most cheating, fraud and desync complaints. Activity 14 - Real-World Anchor: The SouhcNi (Tran Huu Nghia) Reference
Pinned the latency discussion to a real esports reference. The SouhcNi case study on millisecond-level latency jitter in Valorant showed that for competitive players the difference between a good and a great match is literally a few milliseconds. Speakers, students and developers alike could relate to that intuition of ‘feel’ being driven by an engineering number. Activity 15 - Future Trend 1: Edge Computing with Amazon CloudFront
First forward-looking slide. Move game logic closer to the player via edge nodes powered by Amazon CloudFront. Edge computing reduces the hop count and gives the lowest-possible latency for session-based games in the same way CloudFront already does for static content. Activity 16 - Future Trend 2: Cloud Gaming with GPU Servers
Second future vector. Cloud gaming streams full GPU-rendered gameplay using RTP/SRTP protocols under a 100 ms interaction latency budget. Users on low-end devices can play AAA games because all the rendering happens on the cloud side. The infrastructure cost is high, but the addressable market (everyone with a phone) is enormous. Activity 17 - Future Trend 3: Generative AI for Virtual World Creation
Third future vector. Generative AI is used to procedurally create game worlds, quests, characters and dialogue, often via Amazon SageMaker. The infrastructure implication: large bursts of GPU and CPU when an agent generates a new zone, then quiet loads again - a new kind of elastic pattern for cloud providers to support. Activity 18 - Closing Synthesis: Resilience, Latency and Security Harmony
Closed with the integrator’s view. Cloud architecture for modern multiplayer games is a sophisticated ecosystem, not a single service. It requires harmony of resilience (Agones ‘Allocated’ state plus Spot stability via FleetIQ-style prediction), latency optimisation (edge computing, RTT minimisation, CloudFront) and rigorous security auditing (REPs, DynamoDB rate limiting, Kinesis + Athena pipelines). Results and values ​​achieved Lessons learned
A multiplayer game architecture is really two architectures - session-based (low latency, transient) and persistent-world (stateful, AI-heavy). Picking the wrong one is a multi-year mistake; picking correctly makes everything else easier.
Agones shows that a hard problem (Kubernetes scaling down a session mid-match) can be solved with one CRD state - just by introducing an ‘Allocated’ state that Kubernetes will not interrupt. Open-source solutions often beat closed.
Spot instances plus a predictive stability algorithm (FleetIQ-style) save 70 to 80 percent vs on-demand without sacrificing player experience - this is the single most useful cost tool for any student or indie game studio.
Matchmaking is a math problem as much as an engineering problem. The RTT minimisation formula, decentralised Lambda logic and edge-ping detection via DynamoDB together produce the difference between a player who waits 15 seconds and one who waits 3.
The 50 ms latency budget is not a guideline - it is the line. Above it consistently, the game feels broken. Below it, and combined with low jitter, the game feels magical. The esports reference to millisecond-level jitter makes this tangible.
Security for live-service games is best layered edge-to-inside: REPs at the boundary for DDoS, DynamoDB for rate-limited state writes, Kinesis + S3 + Athena for cheater detection. Each layer defends a different threat; skipping any one of them is what shows up in the news.
Newly Acquired Skills
Multiplayer Architecture Reading: ability to read a game company’s job description or tech blog and identify which of the two architectures (session-based vs persistent-world) it runs, which orchestration layer it uses and where its cost optimisation sits.
Game-Tech Cloud Stack Mental Model: a working map of AWS GameLift / FlexMatch / FleetIQ vs Google GKE + Agones + Quilkin vs Azure forecasting vs Oracle Cloud. Lets me pick the right stack on purpose instead of by accident.
Matchmaking Pipeline Mental Model: the ability to sketch a full matchmaking pipeline (edge-ping detection via DynamoDB, JWT-authenticated API Gateway, decentralised Lambda logic, RTT minimisation formula) for any future multiplayer side-project.
Spot + Predicted-Stability Pattern: practical knowledge of how to safely use Spot capacity with a predictive stability algorithm (FleetIQ-style) so cost savings of 70 to 80 percent do not cost us player trust.
Edge-to-Inside Security Pattern: a structured three-layer security shield (REPs at the edge for DDoS, DynamoDB for state-write rate-limiting, Kinesis + S3 + Athena for retrospective cheat detection) ready to copy into future live-service projects.
Soft Skills: sharper listening during a hybrid infrastructure / networking / data-analytics talk; structured note-taking that separated architecture, cost, security, and future-trend sections; clearer questions about realistic student-scale deployments of these patterns.
Closing Note This session re-framed what ‘cloud architecture’ means to me as a student. The argument was not ‘cloud is overhyped, do less of it’, but ‘cloud is overhyped at the level of a single service, do better with an orchestrated ecosystem’. By the end of the talk I had a concrete mental model - two architectural shapes, three elasticity levers, a three-layer security shield - that I can actually take into our group’s next multiplayer prototype on Agones. The clearest take-away is the shift from ‘rent a VM’ to ‘design a resilience + latency + security triangle’: Agones allocates for the session, FleetIQ-style Spot predicts for cost, REPs + DynamoDB + Kinesis defend the trust. That triangle is what I will carry forward to every project that touches live, multi-user systems.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section lists and introduces the blog posts you have shared to AWS Study Group.
Blog 1 - Amazon announces Bedrock AgentCore web search engine for agent-driven web access. This blog explains AWS Web Search on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, a managed search capability for AI agents that delivers up-to-date web knowledge, ranked snippets, source metadata, and zero data egress inside the AWS environment.
Blog 2 - Why and how to migrate to an AWS Network Firewall directly attached to a Transit Gateway (TGW-attached) This blog describes the benefits and migration approach for TGW-attached AWS Network Firewall, including architecture options, cost allocation improvements, phase-based cutover, and key checks for routing and EIP preservation.
Blog 3 - CloudFront in the game backend: Accelerates and protects certain components, but doesn’t replace others? This blog discusses how CloudFront can accelerate and protect web-facing game backend traffic, the API and asset scenarios it fits, and why it is not a replacement for real-time gameplay servers.

title: Build the API and storage layer
content:
Backend foundation In this section, you create the user-facing API layer for the learning journal application. The frontend authenticates with Cognito, then calls API Gateway routes. API Gateway invokes the main Lambda function, and Lambda stores or retrieves data from DynamoDB and S3.
Core responsibilities:
Authenticate users with Cognito and protect routes with a JWT authorizer. Create a Lambda API handler for health checks, profile information, journal CRUD, image presigned URLs, AI job submission, quiz routes, search, and analytics. Create DynamoDB tables for journal logs and AI results. Create an S3 bucket for user-uploaded learning images. Content Configure Cognito and API Gateway Create DynamoDB, S3, and test the API

title: Test the Interface Endpoint
content:
Kết nối các tuyến API với các tác vụ bất đồng bộ Cập nhật Lambda API chính Lambda API chính sẽ trở thành bộ điều phối tác vụ. Nó cần:
Nhận một yêu cầu từ frontend. Tạo một bản ghi trong DynamoDB với status: "pending". Gửi một thông điệp đến hàng đợi SQS phù hợp. Trả về mã định danh tác vụ ngay lập tức cho frontend. Hỗ trợ các tuyến polling để frontend có thể kiểm tra trạng thái tác vụ. Thêm các tuyến báo cáo AI Tạo và bảo vệ các tuyến API Gateway sau:
POST /ai/reports GET /ai/reports GET /ai/reports/{id} POST /ai/reports tạo một mục báo cáo trong AiReports và gửi một thông điệp đến ai-report-jobs. Thông điệp cần bao gồm:
{ "reportId": "rep_...", "userId": "cognito-user-sub", "type": "weekly", "startDate": "2026-07-01", "endDate": "2026-07-07" } Thêm các tuyến bài kiểm tra Tạo và bảo vệ các tuyến API Gateway sau:
GET /quiz POST /quiz GET /quiz/{id} GET /quiz/{id}/attempts POST /quiz/{id}/attempts POST /quiz tạo một mục bài kiểm tra trong Quizzes và gửi một thông điệp đến quiz-jobs. Hãy bao gồm chủ đề, các thẻ đã chọn, khoảng ngày, số lượng câu hỏi và độ khó.
Luồng polling Frontend cần:
Gửi yêu cầu AI. Lưu reportId hoặc quizId được trả về. Gọi tuyến GET liên quan theo chu kỳ mỗi vài giây. Hiển thị trạng thái đang tải khi trạng thái là pending hoặc processing. Hiển thị kết quả khi trạng thái là completed. Hiển thị thông báo thử lại nếu trạng thái là failed.

title: Event 4
content:
Summary Report: “AWS FIRST CLOUD AI JOURNEY COMMUNITY DAY 27/06/2026” 1. AgenticOps Platform - Autonomous Cloud Operations A platform showcase session hosted inside the First Cloud AI Journey community track, delivered by CloudThinker JSC. The talk introduced the company’s positioning as the pioneer ‘VibeOps’ / AgenticOps platform - a system where specialised AI agents autonomously operate, secure and optimise AWS cloud infrastructure 24/7 - and walked through the four-module platform loop (Prevent, Resolve, Predict, Learn), the specialised agents (Cloud, Kubernetes, Security, Database, Super), the AWS integration (Bedrock, SaaS-on-AWS, BYOC), measurable outcomes (MTTR, auto-resolve rate, waste recovery, engineer toil), customer case studies across Telco, FSI, FinTech, Corporate and Manufacturing, and a transparent pricing/deployment model.
Speakers CloudThinker JSC (cloudthinker.io). – Global Reach, Local Expertise - #1 AWS Agentic AI Consulting Service (new, 2026 narrative line). Summary of content and main activities CloudThinker opened with positioning and customer context (Telco, FSI, FinTech, Corporate, Manufacturing), then framed the problem: today’s cloud complexity is not an accident, it is the cumulative result of every right call the team made - and it has produced two visible groans, microservices complexity at scale and the ‘FSI paradox’ (more tools, more engineers, same MTTR). The talk then introduced the Enterprise AgenticOps Platform as a continuous loop of four modules - Prevent (AI Code Review), Resolve (Deep Response), Predict (Cloud Keepers), Learn (MemGraph) - backed by specialised AI agents (Cloud, Kubernetes, Security, Database, Super) running on AWS with Bedrock. The session closed with measurable outcomes (MTTR -87%, 70% auto-resolved, 15-25% cloud waste recovered, engineer toil 40% to <10%, zero-downtime EKS upgrades), case studies (F88, NextPay, Diaflow), a transparent pricing model (Team / Scale / Scale+ / Enterprise) and a deployment choice (CloudThinker Managed SaaS vs. Customer Managed / BYOC), all anchored in the dual pitch line ‘Global Reach - Local Expertise.’
Activity 1 — Brand Introduction: Autonomous Cloud Operations
Opened with the headline ‘Autonomous Cloud Operations.’ Established the company’s promise: specialised AI agents operate, secure and optimise AWS cloud infrastructure 24/7, with no human-in-the-loop for routine work. Set up the rest of the session. Activity 2 — Positioning: Global Reach, Local Expertise
Defined CloudThinker’s market stance as a ‘Global Reach, Local Expertise’ company - international ambition but rooted in the specific realities of Vietnam’s enterprise and FSI customers. Activity 3 — ‘VibeOps’ / AgenticOps Branding (New #1 AWS Agentic AI Service)
Introduced the coined term ‘VibeOps’ as the productisation of AgenticOps - a way of running cloud operations that feels (almost) autonomous. Stressed this is a ’new’ consulting positioning that they want to own in the AWS ecosystem. Activity 4 — Customer Profile Slides (Telco, FSI, FinTech, Corp, Manufacturing)
